inputs.name: airplane_default_presentation
inputs.description:
This input is AIRPLANE (U+2708) with no variation selector. Its renderer
chooses the default presentation. The paired forms append explicit text or
emoji selectors, allowing consumers to test whether apparently similar
values retain distinct display intent and bytes.

Unicode Technical Standard #51 describes presentation style:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr51/#Presentation_Style

inputs.name: airplane_emoji_presentation
inputs.description:
This input is AIRPLANE (U+2708) followed by VARIATION SELECTOR-16 (U+FE0F),
requesting emoji-style presentation. It exercises rendering, equality,
search, editing, and systems that incorrectly discard variation selectors
because they are visually unobtrusive.

Unicode Technical Standard #51 defines emoji presentation sequences:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr51/#Emoji_and_Text_Presentation_Sequences

inputs.name: airplane_text_presentation
inputs.description:
This input is AIRPLANE (U+2708) followed by VARIATION SELECTOR-15 (U+FE0E),
requesting text-style presentation. The invisible selector makes this a
distinct sequence from both the unqualified base and the emoji-qualified
form, even when a particular renderer displays them similarly.

Unicode Technical Standard #51 defines text presentation sequences:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr51/#Emoji_and_Text_Presentation_Sequences

inputs.name: arabic_bismillah_ligature
inputs.description:
This input is ARABIC LIGATURE BISMILLAH AR-RAHMAN AR-RAHEEM (U+FDFD), a
single code point encoding the whole phrase "بسم الله الرحمن الرحيم". It is
one code point and one grapheme cluster, yet renderers draw it with an
enormous advance width, so it stresses any code that assumes display width is
bounded by code-point or grapheme count (column layout, truncation, fixed
buffers, terminal/table rendering). Its East Asian Width property is Neutral,
so width tables that key off that property mispredict its footprint.

Unicode charts the code point in the Arabic Presentation Forms-A block:
https://www.unicode.org/charts/PDF/UFB50.pdf

inputs.name: bidi_arabic_isolate
inputs.description:
This input encloses Arabic text with RIGHT-TO-LEFT ISOLATE (U+2067) and POP
DIRECTIONAL ISOLATE (U+2069) inside left-to-right text. It tests correct
containment of bidirectional ordering around punctuation and surrounding
text, as well as preservation of invisible directional controls.

Unicode Standard Annex #9 defines directional isolates:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr9/#Explicit_Directional_Isolates

inputs.name: bidi_override_role
inputs.description:
This string places RIGHT-TO-LEFT OVERRIDE (U+202E) before the logical ASCII
sequence "resu" and POP DIRECTIONAL FORMATTING (U+202C) after it. Many
renderers therefore display the bounded sequence as "user", making the input
appear similar to its paired ASCII baseline despite different stored bytes.

Unicode Standard Annex #9 defines explicit directional overrides:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr9/

Unicode Technical Report #36 discusses bidi visual spoofing:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr36/tr36-15.html#Bidirectional_Text_Spoofing

inputs.name: bidi_plain_role
inputs.description:
This harmless ASCII string is the logical and visual baseline for the paired
bidirectional-override input. Pairing the two makes it possible to test
whether logs, source viewers, review tools, and sanitizers expose the
difference between stored order and displayed order.

Unicode Standard Annex #9 specifies the Unicode Bidirectional Algorithm:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr9/

inputs.name: canonical_a_with_ring
inputs.description:
This input is LATIN CAPITAL LETTER A WITH RING ABOVE (U+00C5). It is the NFC
result of the paired ANGSTROM SIGN input. The pair catches systems that rely
on binary equality where canonical-equivalent text should compare alike.

Unicode Standard Annex #15 documents singleton canonical equivalence:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr15/

inputs.name: canonical_angstrom_sign
inputs.description:
This input is ANGSTROM SIGN (U+212B). Unicode gives it a canonical
decomposition to LATIN CAPITAL LETTER A WITH RING ABOVE, so it is
canonically equivalent to the paired input despite having a different code
point and UTF-8 encoding.

Unicode Standard Annex #15 documents singleton canonical equivalence:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr15/

inputs.name: canonical_cafe_nfc
inputs.description:
The final character in this input is precomposed LATIN SMALL LETTER E WITH
ACUTE (U+00E9). It looks the same as the paired decomposed form but has a
different code-point sequence and UTF-8 byte representation. Together the
pair tests normalization-aware comparison, hashing, indexing, and storage.

Unicode Standard Annex #15 defines canonical equivalence and normalization:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr15/

inputs.name: canonical_cafe_nfd
inputs.description:
The final visible character in this input is encoded as LATIN SMALL LETTER E
(U+0065) followed by COMBINING ACUTE ACCENT (U+0301). It is canonically
equivalent to the paired precomposed form but differs in code points, bytes,
and naive length calculations.

Unicode Standard Annex #15 defines canonical equivalence and normalization:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr15/

inputs.name: canonical_hangul_jamo_gak
inputs.description:
This input contains HANGUL CHOSEONG KIYEOK (U+1100), JUNGSEONG A (U+1161),
and JONGSEONG KIYEOK (U+11A8). The three-code-point sequence is canonically
equivalent to the paired precomposed Hangul syllable but has different bytes
and naive length.

Unicode Standard Annex #15 specifies algorithmic Hangul composition:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr15/

inputs.name: canonical_hangul_syllable_gak
inputs.description:
This input is the precomposed Hangul syllable GAG (U+AC01), commonly
romanized "gak". It is canonically equivalent to the paired sequence of
three conjoining Jamo, exercising normalization outside Latin text.

Unicode Standard Annex #15 specifies algorithmic Hangul decomposition:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr15/

inputs.name: casing_dotted_i_default_lower
inputs.description:
This input is LATIN SMALL LETTER I (U+0069) followed by COMBINING DOT ABOVE
(U+0307). It is the default full lowercase mapping of the paired precomposed
capital dotted I (U+0130), demonstrating that case conversion can change both
normalization shape and code-point count.

SpecialCasing.txt records this unconditional full lowercase mapping:
https://www.unicode.org/Public/UCD/latest/ucd/SpecialCasing.txt

inputs.name: casing_german_sharp_s
inputs.description:
This input contains LATIN SMALL LETTER SHARP S (U+00DF). Full Unicode
uppercasing of "Straße" produces the paired string "STRASSE", increasing the
number of code points. It tests casing code that assumes every input scalar
maps to exactly one output scalar or that output length cannot grow.

The Unicode Character Database SpecialCasing.txt file records full mappings:
https://www.unicode.org/Public/UCD/latest/ucd/SpecialCasing.txt

inputs.name: casing_german_sharp_s_upper
inputs.description:
This ASCII string is the full uppercase result of the paired German word
containing LATIN SMALL LETTER SHARP S (U+00DF). It is a useful baseline for
verifying length-changing uppercase conversion and case-insensitive lookup.

The Unicode Character Database SpecialCasing.txt file records full mappings:
https://www.unicode.org/Public/UCD/latest/ucd/SpecialCasing.txt

inputs.name: casing_greek_final_sigma
inputs.description:
This uppercase Greek word ends in CAPITAL SIGMA (U+03A3). Lowercasing must
use FINAL SIGMA (U+03C2) in this context. The paired word places sigma before
another cased letter, requiring ordinary SMALL SIGMA (U+03C3) instead.

SpecialCasing.txt records the context-sensitive Final_Sigma rule:
https://www.unicode.org/Public/UCD/latest/ucd/SpecialCasing.txt

inputs.name: casing_greek_nonfinal_sigma
inputs.description:
In this uppercase Greek word, CAPITAL SIGMA (U+03A3) is followed by another
cased letter, so lowercasing must produce ordinary SMALL SIGMA (U+03C3).
Pairing it with a word-final sigma example tests context-aware conversion.

SpecialCasing.txt records the context-sensitive Final_Sigma rule:
https://www.unicode.org/Public/UCD/latest/ucd/SpecialCasing.txt

inputs.name: casing_turkic_capital_dotted_i
inputs.description:
This input is LATIN CAPITAL LETTER I WITH DOT ABOVE (U+0130). In Turkish and
Azeri it lowercases to the paired ASCII "i"; under the default Unicode full
mapping it lowercases to a two-code-point sequence instead. It exercises both
locale-sensitive and length-changing casing.

SpecialCasing.txt records the conditional and full mappings:
https://www.unicode.org/Public/UCD/latest/ucd/SpecialCasing.txt

inputs.name: casing_turkic_capital_i
inputs.description:
This input is LATIN CAPITAL LETTER I (U+0049). In Turkish and Azeri casing it
lowercases to the paired DOTLESS SMALL I (U+0131), rather than ASCII "i".
The pair tests locale-sensitive case conversion and identifier matching.

SpecialCasing.txt records the language-sensitive Turkic mappings:
https://www.unicode.org/Public/UCD/latest/ucd/SpecialCasing.txt

inputs.name: casing_turkic_dotless_i
inputs.description:
This input is LATIN SMALL LETTER DOTLESS I (U+0131), the Turkish and Azeri
lowercase counterpart of the paired ASCII capital "I". It exposes systems
that apply language-neutral or English-only casing rules to Turkic text.

SpecialCasing.txt records the language-sensitive Turkic mappings:
https://www.unicode.org/Public/UCD/latest/ucd/SpecialCasing.txt

inputs.name: casing_turkic_small_i
inputs.description:
This input is LATIN SMALL LETTER I (U+0069), the Turkish and Azeri lowercase
counterpart of the paired dotted capital I (U+0130). It is included as the
locale-specific baseline for testing language-aware case conversion.

SpecialCasing.txt records the language-sensitive Turkic mappings:
https://www.unicode.org/Public/UCD/latest/ucd/SpecialCasing.txt

inputs.name: combining_mark_overflow
inputs.description:
This is a single base letter "e" (U+0065) followed by thirty COMBINING
DIACRITICAL MARKS drawn from the U+0300 block, alternating above and below the
baseline. All thirty-one code points form one grapheme cluster, yet the stacked
marks grow far taller than the font's line box and bleed into the lines above
and below — the "Zalgo" effect — so naive layout that assumes a glyph stays
within its em box will overlap neighbouring rows. It also stresses cluster
segmentation, cursor movement, and combining-mark normalisation.

Unicode Standard Annex #29 keeps a base and its trailing combining marks in a
single grapheme cluster: https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr29/

inputs.name: confusable_admin_cyrillic
inputs.description:
The first character in this identifier is CYRILLIC SMALL LETTER A (U+0430);
the remaining four characters are Latin ASCII. It can look like the paired
all-Latin identifier while remaining a different string in every binary
comparison.

The pair exercises identifier restrictions, account-name checks, audit
displays, and spoof-resistant matching. Unicode Technical Standard #39
defines mixed-script confusable detection:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr39/#Confusable_Detection

inputs.name: confusable_admin_latin
inputs.description:
This all-Latin ASCII identifier is the baseline for a paired mixed-script
lookalike. Its initial character is LATIN SMALL LETTER A (U+0061), which is
visually similar in many fonts to the Cyrillic character used by the pair.

Unicode Technical Standard #39 defines confusable detection:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr39/#Confusable_Detection

inputs.name: confusable_paypal_cyrillic
inputs.description:
This identifier uses Cyrillic U+0440, U+0430, U+0443, U+0440, and U+0430
followed by Latin "l" (U+006C). It can resemble the paired ASCII identifier
in common fonts while remaining unequal by code points and bytes.

The pair exercises account-name policy, search, allowlists, duplicate
detection, audit display, and spoof-resistant comparison. Unicode Technical
Standard #39 defines mixed-script confusable detection:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr39/#Confusable_Detection

inputs.name: confusable_paypal_latin
inputs.description:
This all-Latin ASCII identifier is the baseline for the paired mixed-script
lookalike. Although the two may appear nearly identical in some fonts, this
input uses only U+0070, U+0061, U+0079, U+0070, U+0061, and U+006C.

Unicode Technical Standard #39 defines confusable detection:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr39/#Confusable_Detection

inputs.name: effective_power
inputs.description:
This input file caused a boot-loop in iphones due to an issue handling
unicode combining characters.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SpringBoard#%22effective_power%22_bug

inputs.name: emoji_zwj_kiss_skin_tones
inputs.description:
This is the RGI emoji ZWJ sequence "kiss: woman, man, medium-light skin tone,
dark skin tone": WOMAN (U+1F469) + EMOJI MODIFIER FITZPATRICK TYPE-3 (U+1F3FC),
ZERO WIDTH JOINER (U+200D), HEAVY BLACK HEART (U+2764) + VARIATION SELECTOR-16
(U+FE0F), ZWJ, KISS MARK (U+1F48B), ZWJ, MAN (U+1F468) + EMOJI MODIFIER
FITZPATRICK TYPE-6 (U+1F3FF). Ten code points collapse into a single glyph
occupying roughly two columns when the font carries the sequence, but a font
that lacks it falls back to rendering each component separately, so the same
bytes occupy anywhere from one to seven-plus emoji of width. It exercises
width estimation, fallback rendering, and the most-scalars-per-glyph corner.

Unicode Technical Standard #51 defines emoji ZWJ sequences and skin-tone
modifiers: https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr51/#Emoji_ZWJ_Sequences

inputs.name: float_f16_epsilon
inputs.description:
This is binary16 machine epsilon, 2^-10: the gap between 1 and the next
representable value above 1. It catches code that assumes a universal
epsilon across float widths, uses epsilon as an absolute tolerance at every
magnitude, or accidentally performs the calculation at a wider precision.

inputs.name: float_f16_largest_subnormal
inputs.description:
This is the largest binary16 subnormal, immediately below the smallest
positive normal. The adjacent pair crosses the encoding boundary where the
implicit leading significand bit appears, exposing normalization, conversion,
rounding, and off-by-one errors near gradual underflow.

inputs.name: float_f16_max_finite
inputs.description:
This is the largest finite binary16 value, 65504. Operations that round above
this range may produce positive infinity, saturate, trap, or be widened by an
implementation before rounding. Pairing it with infinity targets overflow
checks that rely on ordinary finite comparisons.

inputs.name: float_f16_negative_zero
inputs.description:
IEEE 754 binary16 negative zero has only the sign bit set. Numeric equality
normally treats it as equal to positive zero, while 1 / -0 produces negative
infinity under the default IEEE rules. Serialization, hashing, formatting,
and sign-sensitive algorithms may therefore expose its distinct bit pattern.

inputs.name: float_f16_one
inputs.description:
This exact binary16 value is a baseline operand paired with both signed
zeros. Dividing it by those inputs tests divide-by-zero handling: IEEE 754
default arithmetic returns signed infinity, while runtimes, validators, or
enabled traps may instead report an error. It is also paired with the next
representable value above one to test precision-sensitive comparisons.

inputs.name: float_f16_one_plus_epsilon
inputs.description:
This is the binary16 value immediately above 1, exactly 1 + 2^-10. The pair
differs by one unit in the last place, so it probes approximate-equality
thresholds, lossy promotion or serialization, and displays that round both
values to the same short decimal string.

inputs.name: float_f16_one_tenth
inputs.description:
This is the nearest binary16 representation of decimal 0.1, whose exact
value is 0.0999755859375. The paired f32 and f64 encodings are each different
approximations of the same decimal literal, exposing cross-width equality,
promotion, serialization, and tolerance assumptions.

inputs.name: float_f16_positive_infinity
inputs.description:
Positive infinity is a valid IEEE 754 binary16 value rather than a finite
number or a NaN. It tests parsers and serializers that reject non-finite
values, range checks that only compare against a maximum, and arithmetic such
as infinity minus infinity that produces NaN.

inputs.name: float_f16_positive_zero
inputs.description:
IEEE 754 binary16 positive zero has all bits clear. It compares equal to
negative zero, but its sign affects division, reciprocals, copysign, and some
branch-cut functions. It is also paired with the smallest subnormal to expose
implementations that flush tiny non-zero values to zero.

inputs.name: float_f16_quiet_nan
inputs.description:
This is a quiet NaN with the conventional quiet bit set and a zero payload.
NaN compares unequal to every value, including itself, and unordered
comparisons, hashing, sorting, deduplication, and serialization frequently
mishandle that rule or canonicalize its representation.

inputs.name: float_f16_signaling_nan
inputs.description:
This is a signaling NaN with payload 1. Merely loading, converting, or
performing arithmetic on it may raise the invalid-operation flag and quiet
the NaN, so the original bits are not guaranteed to survive a round trip
through a floating-point register or higher-level numeric API.

inputs.name: float_f16_smallest_positive_normal
inputs.description:
This is the smallest positive normal binary16 value, 2^-14. It is adjacent
to the largest subnormal despite using a different exponent/significand
interpretation, making the pair useful for testing decoders, nextafter,
ordering, and underflow thresholds.

inputs.name: float_f16_smallest_positive_subnormal
inputs.description:
This is the smallest positive non-zero binary16 value, 2^-24. Hardware or
accelerated kernels using flush-to-zero modes may turn it into positive zero,
changing equality, loop termination, underflow behavior, and division
results. Its encoding also exercises gradual underflow.

This is binary32 machine epsilon, 2^-23: the gap between 1 and the next
representable value above 1. It catches code that assumes a universal
epsilon across float widths, uses epsilon as an absolute tolerance at every
magnitude, or accidentally performs the calculation at a wider precision.

termination, underflow behavior, and division results.

inputs.name: float_parsing_c_hex_f16_one_tenth
inputs.description:
This ISO C hexadecimal floating subject sequence exactly represents the
binary16 value nearest decimal 0.1. Hexadecimal syntax avoids decimal
conversion ambiguity and tests parsers that support C strtod-style forms but
accidentally round through binary32 or binary64 before narrowing.

ISO C11 section 7.22.1.3 specifies the strtod family:
https://www.open-std.org/jtc1/sc22/wg14/www/docs/n1570.pdf

inputs.name: float_parsing_c_hex_f32_smallest_subnormal
inputs.description:
This ISO C hexadecimal floating subject sequence exactly represents the
smallest positive binary32 subnormal, 2^-149. It probes hexadecimal parsing,
gradual underflow, range reporting, and implementations that flush the
correctly parsed result to zero.

ISO C11 section 7.22.1.3 specifies hexadecimal strtod forms:
https://www.open-std.org/jtc1/sc22/wg14/www/docs/n1570.pdf

inputs.name: float_parsing_c_hex_f64_smallest_subnormal
inputs.description:
This ISO C hexadecimal floating subject sequence exactly represents the
smallest positive binary64 subnormal, 2^-1074. It targets exponent-range
boundaries, gradual underflow, correct range-error signaling, and parsers
that accidentally treat the value as zero.

ISO C11 section 7.22.1.3 specifies hexadecimal strtod forms:
https://www.open-std.org/jtc1/sc22/wg14/www/docs/n1570.pdf

inputs.name: float_parsing_c_hex_without_exponent
inputs.description:
The ISO C strtod subject-sequence grammar permits this hexadecimal form
without a binary exponent and interprets it as 1.5. In contrast, a
hexadecimal floating constant in C source requires a p/P exponent. This
distinction catches parsers that reuse the language lexer too literally.

ISO C11 sections 6.4.4.2 and 7.22.1.3 define the two related grammars:
https://www.open-std.org/jtc1/sc22/wg14/www/docs/n1570.pdf

inputs.name: float_parsing_c_nan_payload
inputs.description:
This is an ISO C NaN subject sequence with an implementation-defined
n-char-sequence payload. A conforming strtod-family parser recognizes the
overall form, but payload interpretation, preservation, and resulting NaN
bits can vary. It also tests tokenization through the closing parenthesis.

ISO C11 section 7.22.1.3 defines NAN(n-char-sequence):
https://www.open-std.org/jtc1/sc22/wg14/www/docs/n1570.pdf

inputs.name: float_parsing_ecmascript_hex_max_safe_integer
inputs.description:
ECMAScript StringToNumber accepts non-decimal integer literals in strings.
This hexadecimal value is 2^53 - 1, the largest integer that binary64 can
represent exactly throughout ordinary arithmetic. It tests prefix handling,
exact integer conversion, and parsers that only implement decimal syntax.

ECMA-262 defines non-decimal StringNumericLiteral forms here:
https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/abstract-operations.html#sec-stringtonumber

inputs.name: float_parsing_ecmascript_leading_zeroes
inputs.description:
ECMAScript StringToNumber accepts this decimal StringNumericLiteral with many
leading zeroes and produces 1.5. The same text is not a valid RFC 8259 JSON
number because JSON forbids leading zeroes. It targets accidental grammar
sharing between JavaScript coercion, source literals, and JSON parsers.

ECMA-262 defines StringNumericLiteral and StringToNumber here:
https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/abstract-operations.html#sec-stringtonumber

inputs.name: float_parsing_json_f16_halfway
inputs.description:
This valid JSON decimal is exactly halfway between binary16 1.0 and the next
representable value, 1.0009765625. Round-to-nearest, ties-to-even selects
1.0; double rounding or a parser using another tie rule can select the upper
neighbor instead.

RFC 8259 defines the JSON number syntax:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8259.html#section-6

inputs.name: float_parsing_json_f32_halfway
inputs.description:
This valid JSON decimal is exactly halfway between binary32 1.0 and the next
representable value, 1.00000011920928955078125. Correct round-to-nearest,
ties-to-even selects 1.0, making the input useful for detecting incorrect
tie handling and double rounding.

RFC 8259 defines the JSON number syntax:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8259.html#section-6

inputs.name: float_parsing_json_f64_halfway
inputs.description:
This valid JSON decimal is exactly halfway between binary64 1.0 and its next
representable value. Correct round-to-nearest, ties-to-even selects 1.0. Its
long exact significand exposes parsers that first round through an
insufficient decimal or binary intermediate.

RFC 8259 defines the JSON number syntax:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8259.html#section-6

inputs.name: float_parsing_json_large_exponent
inputs.description:
This is syntactically valid under RFC 8259 even though its mathematical
value exceeds every finite IEEE 754 binary16, binary32, and binary64 value.
Parsers differ between returning positive infinity, reporting a range error,
retaining an arbitrary-precision decimal, or rejecting the document.

RFC 8259 specifically identifies 1E400 as an interoperability risk:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8259.html#section-6

inputs.name: float_parsing_json_long_pi
inputs.description:
This valid JSON number carries substantially more precision than binary64.
It exercises correct decimal rounding, arbitrary-precision handoff, and
implementations that truncate the significand before conversion. RFC 8259
uses this exact value as an example of a potential interoperability problem.

RFC 8259 section 6 discusses precision beyond binary64:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8259.html#section-6

inputs.name: float_parsing_json_negative_zero
inputs.description:
This is a valid RFC 8259 JSON number containing an integer minus sign and
zero. IEEE 754 parsers can preserve it as negative zero, while intermediate
decimal, integer, or JSON data models may normalize it to positive zero and
lose sign-sensitive behavior such as the result of a reciprocal.

RFC 8259 section 6 defines the JSON number grammar:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8259.html#section-6

inputs.name: float_parsing_json_tiny_exponent
inputs.description:
This RFC 8259 number is non-zero mathematically but is below the smallest
positive subnormal in binary16, binary32, and binary64. It targets parsers
that silently underflow to zero, preserve an arbitrary-precision decimal,
report range loss, or mishandle a long signed decimal exponent.

RFC 8259 permits implementations to limit numeric range and precision:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc8259.html#section-6

inputs.name: float_parsing_positive_infinity
inputs.description:
This spelling is accepted as positive infinity by ISO C strtod-family
functions, Python float(), and ECMAScript StringToNumber. It is deliberately
not a valid RFC 8259 JSON number. The input targets APIs that silently switch
grammars or apply a finite-only policy after otherwise successful parsing.

The relevant parsing grammars are documented here:
https://www.open-std.org/jtc1/sc22/wg14/www/docs/n1570.pdf
https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#float
https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/abstract-operations.html#sec-stringtonumber

inputs.name: float_parsing_python_digit_separators
inputs.description:
Python float() permits single underscores between decimal digits in both the
integer and fractional parts. This valid value exercises parsers that confuse
Python's string grammar with JSON or C strtod, strip separators too broadly,
or accept malformed repeated and edge-position underscores.

Python documents the floatvalue grammar here:
https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#float

inputs.name: float_parsing_python_leading_decimal_point
inputs.description:
Python float() accepts a fractional value with no digit before the decimal
point. This is valid Python floatvalue syntax but invalid JSON number syntax,
making it useful for finding parsers whose accepted grammar is broader or
narrower than their documented interface.

Python documents the floatvalue grammar here:
https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#float

inputs.name: float_parsing_python_trailing_decimal_point
inputs.description:
Python float() accepts a number ending immediately after its decimal point.
RFC 8259 requires at least one fractional digit, so this compact input helps
distinguish a Python-compatible parser from a strict JSON parser and catches
tokenizers that always require digits on both sides of a decimal point.

Python documents the floatvalue grammar here:
https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#float

inputs.name: float_parsing_python_unicode_digits
inputs.description:
Python float() defines a digit as any Unicode character in general category
Nd, so these Arabic-Indic digits form the valid value 123.5. ASCII-only
parsers will reject it, while careless byte-oriented normalization can split
the multibyte UTF-8 digits or produce a different token.

Python documents Unicode decimal digits in the floatvalue grammar:
https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#float

inputs.name: fullwidth_latin_letters
inputs.description:
This is the word "HELLO" written with FULLWIDTH LATIN CAPITAL LETTERS
(U+FF28 U+FF25 U+FF2C U+FF2C U+FF2F). Each is a single code point and grapheme,
but its East Asian Width property is Fullwidth, so a monospace terminal or
fixed-cell grid allocates two columns per character. Code that measures the
string by code-point or grapheme count assumes five columns and under-counts
the real width by half, misaligning tables, padding, and truncation.

Unicode Standard Annex #11 defines the East Asian Width property:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr11/

inputs.name: grapheme_combining_acute
inputs.description:
This single displayed grapheme is LATIN SMALL LETTER E (U+0065) followed by
COMBINING ACUTE ACCENT (U+0301). It tests code that mistakes code points or
UTF-8 bytes for user-perceived characters during deletion, truncation,
cursor movement, regular-expression matching, or length validation.

Unicode Standard Annex #29 defines extended grapheme cluster boundaries:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr29/

inputs.name: grapheme_emoji_modifier
inputs.description:
This single emoji grapheme combines THUMBS UP SIGN (U+1F44D) with EMOJI
MODIFIER FITZPATRICK TYPE-4 (U+1F3FD). It is two scalar values and eight UTF-8
bytes, exposing implementations that split the modifier from its base or
impose limits using the wrong notion of string length.

Unicode Technical Standard #51 defines emoji modifier sequences:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr51/#Emoji_Modifiers

inputs.name: grapheme_family_zwj
inputs.description:
This single family emoji is a seven-code-point sequence containing four
person emoji joined by three ZERO WIDTH JOINER characters (U+200D). It
stresses grapheme segmentation, cursor movement, deletion, display width,
truncation, and renderer fallback more heavily than a simple ZWJ pair.

Unicode Technical Standard #51 defines emoji ZWJ sequences:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr51/#Emoji_ZWJ_Sequences

inputs.name: grapheme_gb_flag
inputs.description:
This single flag grapheme consists of REGIONAL INDICATOR SYMBOL LETTER G
(U+1F1EC) followed by LETTER B (U+1F1E7). Flag display depends on treating
the pair as one cluster; splitting or reordering the indicators changes both
rendering and meaning.

Unicode Technical Standard #51 defines valid emoji flag sequences:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr51/#Flags

inputs.name: grapheme_keycap_one
inputs.description:
This single keycap grapheme contains DIGIT ONE (U+0031), VARIATION
SELECTOR-16 (U+FE0F), and COMBINING ENCLOSING KEYCAP (U+20E3). It looks like
one symbol while combining an ASCII base, an invisible presentation
selector, and a combining mark.

Unicode Technical Standard #51 defines emoji keycap sequences:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr51/#Emoji_Sequences

inputs.name: grapheme_woman_technologist
inputs.description:
This single emoji grapheme joins WOMAN (U+1F469) and PERSONAL COMPUTER
(U+1F4BB) with ZERO WIDTH JOINER (U+200D). Renderers commonly show one
pictograph although the input contains three scalar values, making it useful
for segmentation, editing, width, and fallback-rendering tests.

Unicode Technical Standard #51 defines emoji ZWJ sequences:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr51/#Emoji_ZWJ_Sequences

inputs.name: heart_default_presentation
inputs.description:
This input is BLACK HEART SUIT (U+2665) with no variation selector. Its
presentation is therefore determined by Unicode defaults and the renderer.
Compare it with the paired text- and emoji-presentation sequences, which may
look different despite sharing the same base character.

Unicode Technical Standard #51 describes presentation style:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr51/#Presentation_Style

inputs.name: heart_emoji_presentation
inputs.description:
This input is BLACK HEART SUIT (U+2665) followed by VARIATION SELECTOR-16
(U+FE0F), requesting emoji-style presentation. It tests whether systems
preserve meaningful default-ignorable characters and distinguish display
intent from the underlying base character.

Unicode Technical Standard #51 defines emoji presentation sequences:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr51/#Emoji_and_Text_Presentation_Sequences

inputs.name: heart_text_presentation
inputs.description:
This input is BLACK HEART SUIT (U+2665) followed by VARIATION SELECTOR-15
(U+FE0E), requesting text-style presentation. The selector is normally
invisible but changes the encoded sequence and can affect rendering, search,
equality, cursor movement, and sanitization.

Unicode Technical Standard #51 defines text presentation sequences:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr51/#Emoji_and_Text_Presentation_Sequences

inputs.name: integer_i128_maximum
inputs.description:
This is the maximum i128 value, 170141183460469231731687303715884105727. Adding 1 produces the
unrepresentable value 170141183460469231731687303715884105728 and may trap, wrap to -170141183460469231731687303715884105728, saturate,
or be evaluated in a wider type. The same bit pattern is the last u128
value that converts to i128 without changing the mathematical value.

inputs.name: integer_i128_minimum
inputs.description:
This is the minimum i128 value, -170141183460469231731687303715884105728. Its positive magnitude,
170141183460469231731687303715884105728, is not representable as an i128, so negation, absolute value,
and division by -1 may overflow, trap, wrap, or require a wider result type.
Subtracting 1 also crosses the lower arithmetic boundary.

inputs.name: integer_i128_negative_one
inputs.description:
This is -1, represented with every bit set in two's-complement i128. It
is the divisor that makes i128 minimum division overflow and exposes
arithmetic-versus-logical shifts, sign extension, and signed-to-unsigned
conversion. The same bytes represent u128 maximum.

inputs.name: integer_i128_one
inputs.description:
One is a baseline i128 operand for boundary arithmetic. Adding it to the
maximum overflows, subtracting it from the minimum underflows, and dividing it
by zero exercises integer divide-by-zero handling.

inputs.name: integer_i128_sign_extended_i64_minimum
inputs.description:
This is i64 minimum represented as i128, requiring the upper
64 bits to be set during sign extension. It catches zero-extension,
partial-register, and widening-conversion bugs while preserving the value
-9223372036854775808.

inputs.name: integer_i128_zero
inputs.description:
Zero is the i128 additive identity and the critical invalid divisor for
integer division and remainder. Pairing it with one exercises divide-by-zero
handling across fixed-width integer implementations.

inputs.name: integer_i16_binary16_first_lost_negative

inputs.name: integer_i16_maximum

inputs.name: integer_i8_maximum

This is the maximum i8 value, 127. Adding 1 produces the unrepresentable value
128 and may trap, wrap to -128, saturate, or be evaluated in a wider type.
Its all-lower-bits-set encoding is also the last u8 value that can be converted

inputs.name: integer_i8_minimum
inputs.description:
This is the minimum i8 value, -128. Its positive magnitude, 128, is not
representable as an i8, so unary negation and absolute-value operations may
overflow, trap, wrap, or require a wider result type. Dividing it by -1 also
has the mathematically correct but unrepresentable quotient 128, while
subtracting 1 crosses the lower arithmetic boundary.

inputs.name: integer_i8_negative_one
inputs.description:
This is -1, represented with every bit set in two's-complement i8. It is the
divisor that makes i8 minimum division overflow, and it commonly exposes

and signed-to-unsigned conversion. The same byte represents u8 maximum.

inputs.name: integer_u128_above_u64_maximum
inputs.description:
This is one above u64 maximum, represented as u128. Narrowing it to
u64 may reject, saturate, or wrap to zero. It also catches serializers,
FFI layers, and intermediate expressions that silently retain only the lower
64 bits.

inputs.name: integer_u128_alternating_ones
inputs.description:
This checkerboard bit pattern alternates 1 and 0 across the entire u128
value and has the sign bit set. Together with its complement it catches stuck
bits, incorrect masks, shift errors, and signed intermediates.

inputs.name: integer_u128_alternating_zeroes
inputs.description:
This checkerboard bit pattern is the complement of the paired alternating-one
value. Its high bit is clear, so the pair also exposes code that accidentally
changes behavior when an unsigned value crosses the signed boundary.

inputs.name: integer_u128_binary64_lower_word_lost
inputs.description:
This is 2^64 + 1. Conversion to binary64 rounds it to 2^64, so converting
back loses the low-order one bit and makes the entire lower 64-bit word zero.
It is a compact canary for routing 128-bit identifiers, counters, or database
values through a double-precision intermediate.

inputs.name: integer_u128_byte_order
inputs.description:
Every byte in this u128 value is distinct. The little-endian encoding is
therefore visibly ordered and catches whole-value byte reversal, swapped
16/32/64-bit words, partial loads, and accidental truncation.

inputs.name: integer_u128_high_bit
inputs.description:
This is 170141183460469231731687303715884105728, the smallest u128 value with the high bit set and the
first value not representable as i128. Reinterpreting the same bytes as
i128 produces -170141183460469231731687303715884105728; checked conversion may reject it.

inputs.name: integer_u128_maximum
inputs.description:
This is the maximum u128 value, 340282366920938463463374607431768211455, with every bit set.
Adding 1 crosses the unsigned upper boundary and may trap, wrap to zero,
saturate, or be widened. Reinterpreting the same bytes as i128 produces
-1, exposing signedness mistakes in storage, comparison, and conversion.

inputs.name: integer_u128_minimum
inputs.description:
This is the minimum u128 value, 0. Subtracting 1 crosses the unsigned
lower boundary and may trap, wrap to 340282366920938463463374607431768211455, saturate, or be widened.
It is also an invalid divisor for integer division and remainder.

inputs.name: integer_u128_one
inputs.description:
One is a baseline u128 operand for boundary arithmetic. Adding it to the
maximum overflows, subtracting it from zero underflows, and dividing it by zero
exercises integer divide-by-zero handling.

inputs.name: integer_u128_signed_maximum
inputs.description:
This is 170141183460469231731687303715884105727, the greatest u128 value also representable as
i128. Adding 1 sets the high bit and reaches 170141183460469231731687303715884105728, the first u128
value outside the i128 positive range. The pair targets off-by-one range
checks, narrowing conversions, and sign-bit confusion.

inputs.name: integer_u128_u64_maximum
inputs.description:
This is u64 maximum represented as u128. It is the last value that
survives narrowing to u64; the paired next value requires bit 64
and exposes truncation, masking, and arithmetic performed at the wrong width.

inputs.name: integer_u16_binary16_exact_boundary
inputs.description:
This is 2^11. Binary16 represents it exactly, but the spacing between
adjacent representable values becomes 2 at this magnitude. It is therefore
exact itself but is the first positive integer beyond the all-integers-exact
range.

inputs.name: integer_u16_binary16_first_lost_integer
inputs.description:
This is 2^11 + 1, the first positive integer that does not survive conversion
to binary16 and back. Round-to-nearest maps it to 2^11, exposing silent
precision loss in reduced-precision storage, accelerators, and interchange
formats.

inputs.name: integer_u16_binary16_max_safe_integer
inputs.description:
This is 2^11 - 1, the largest integer for which every integer through that
magnitude is distinct in IEEE 754 binary16. The next integer is exactly
representable but no longer safe because binary16 has only 11 bits of
significand precision.

inputs.name: integer_u16_high_bit
inputs.description:
This is 32768, the smallest u16 value with the high bit set and the first
value that cannot be represented as i16. Reinterpreting the same bytes as i16
produces -32768, while narrowing or checked conversion may reject it. The
value also exposes accidental sign extension and signed comparisons.

inputs.name: integer_u16_maximum

inputs.name: integer_u16_signed_maximum

inputs.name: integer_u32_binary32_first_lost_integer

inputs.name: integer_u32_binary32_max_safe_integer

magnitude is distinct in IEEE 754 binary32. The next integer is exactly
representable but no longer safe because binary32 has 24 bits of significand

inputs.name: integer_u32_deadbeef
inputs.description:
This is 0xDEADBEEF, a conspicuous sentinel and poison value used in debugging
and memory diagnostics. It is also asymmetric by byte, so it exposes byte-order
mistakes while remaining immediately recognizable in logs and dumps.

The Linux kernel includes 0xDEADBEEF among its poison values:
https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/master/include/linux/poison.h

inputs.name: integer_u32_high_bit
inputs.description:
This is 2147483648, the smallest u32 value with the high bit set and the
first value not representable as i32. Reinterpreting the same bytes as
i32 produces -2147483648; checked conversion may reject it.

inputs.name: integer_u32_maximum
inputs.description:
This is the maximum u32 value, 4294967295, with every bit set.
Adding 1 crosses the unsigned upper boundary and may trap, wrap to zero,
saturate, or be widened. Reinterpreting the same bytes as i32 produces
-1, exposing signedness mistakes in storage, comparison, and conversion.

inputs.name: integer_u32_minimum
inputs.description:
This is the minimum u32 value, 0. Subtracting 1 crosses the unsigned
lower boundary and may trap, wrap to 4294967295, saturate, or be widened.
It is also an invalid divisor for integer division and remainder.

inputs.name: integer_u32_msvc_freed_fill
inputs.description:
This repeated 0xdd byte is used by the Microsoft CRT debug heap for freed
blocks retained by the debug heap. Seeing it as an integer is a strong canary
for stale or use-after-free data crossing a typed boundary.

Microsoft documents the debug-heap fill values here:
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/c-runtime-library/crt-debug-heap-details

inputs.name: integer_u32_msvc_guard_fill
inputs.description:
This repeated 0xfd byte is used by the Microsoft CRT debug heap for guard
regions around allocations. Seeing it as an integer is a strong canary for
an out-of-bounds read crossing a typed boundary.

Microsoft documents the debug-heap fill values here:
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/c-runtime-library/crt-debug-heap-details

inputs.name: integer_u32_msvc_new_fill
inputs.description:
This repeated 0xcd byte is used by the Microsoft CRT debug heap for newly
allocated objects. Seeing it as an integer is a strong canary for
uninitialized, stale, or out-of-bounds data crossing a typed boundary.

Microsoft documents the debug-heap fill values here:
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/cpp/c-runtime-library/crt-debug-heap-details

inputs.name: integer_u32_one
inputs.description:
One is a baseline u32 operand for boundary arithmetic. Adding it to the
maximum overflows, subtracting it from zero underflows, and dividing it by zero
exercises integer divide-by-zero handling.

inputs.name: integer_u32_signed_maximum
inputs.description:
This is 2147483647, the greatest u32 value also representable as
i32. Adding 1 sets the high bit and reaches 2147483648, the first u32
value outside the i32 positive range. The pair targets off-by-one range
checks, narrowing conversions, and sign-bit confusion.

inputs.name: integer_u32_u16_maximum
inputs.description:
This is u16 maximum represented as u32. It is the last value that
survives narrowing to u16; the paired next value requires bit 16
and exposes truncation, masking, and arithmetic performed at the wrong width.

inputs.name: integer_u64_above_u32_maximum
inputs.description:
This is one above u32 maximum, represented as u64. Narrowing it to
u32 may reject, saturate, or wrap to zero. It also catches serializers,
FFI layers, and intermediate expressions that silently retain only the lower
32 bits.

inputs.name: integer_u64_alternating_ones
inputs.description:
This checkerboard bit pattern alternates 1 and 0 across the entire u64
value and has the sign bit set. Together with its complement it catches stuck
bits, incorrect masks, shift errors, and signed intermediates.

inputs.name: integer_u64_alternating_zeroes
inputs.description:
This checkerboard bit pattern is the complement of the paired alternating-one
value. Its high bit is clear, so the pair also exposes code that accidentally
changes behavior when an unsigned value crosses the signed boundary.

inputs.name: integer_u64_binary64_exact_boundary
inputs.description:
This is 2^53. Binary64 represents it exactly, but the spacing between
adjacent representable values becomes 2 at this magnitude. It is a critical
boundary for JSON, JavaScript, and other systems that route integers through
an IEEE 754 binary64 value.

ECMAScript defines the safe-integer limit in Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER:
https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/numbers-and-dates.html#sec-number.max_safe_integer

inputs.name: integer_u64_binary64_first_lost_integer
inputs.description:
This is 2^53 + 1, the first positive integer that cannot be represented
exactly as binary64; conversion rounds it to 2^53 under round-to-nearest. It
catches silent precision loss in JSON, JavaScript, database, and FFI paths.

inputs.name: integer_u64_binary64_max_safe_integer
inputs.description:
This is 2^53 - 1, the largest integer for which every integer through that
magnitude is distinct in binary64. The next integer is exactly representable
but is no longer safe because it shares a binary64 neighborhood with values
that cannot be distinguished.

ECMAScript specifies this value as Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER:
https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/numbers-and-dates.html#sec-number.max_safe_integer

inputs.name: integer_u64_byte_order
inputs.description:
Every byte in this u64 value is distinct. The little-endian encoding is
therefore visibly ordered and catches whole-value byte reversal, swapped
16/32/64-bit words, partial loads, and accidental truncation.

inputs.name: integer_u64_high_bit
inputs.description:
This is 9223372036854775808, the smallest u64 value with the high bit set and the
first value not representable as i64. Reinterpreting the same bytes as
i64 produces -9223372036854775808; checked conversion may reject it.

inputs.name: integer_u64_maximum
inputs.description:
This is the maximum u64 value, 18446744073709551615, with every bit set.
Adding 1 crosses the unsigned upper boundary and may trap, wrap to zero,
saturate, or be widened. Reinterpreting the same bytes as i64 produces
-1, exposing signedness mistakes in storage, comparison, and conversion.

inputs.name: integer_u64_minimum
inputs.description:
This is the minimum u64 value, 0. Subtracting 1 crosses the unsigned
lower boundary and may trap, wrap to 18446744073709551615, saturate, or be widened.
It is also an invalid divisor for integer division and remainder.

inputs.name: integer_u64_one
inputs.description:
One is a baseline u64 operand for boundary arithmetic. Adding it to the
maximum overflows, subtracting it from zero underflows, and dividing it by zero
exercises integer divide-by-zero handling.

inputs.name: integer_u64_signed_maximum
inputs.description:
This is 9223372036854775807, the greatest u64 value also representable as
i64. Adding 1 sets the high bit and reaches 9223372036854775808, the first u64
value outside the i64 positive range. The pair targets off-by-one range
checks, narrowing conversions, and sign-bit confusion.

inputs.name: integer_u64_u32_maximum
inputs.description:
This is u32 maximum represented as u64. It is the last value that
survives narrowing to u32; the paired next value requires bit 32
and exposes truncation, masking, and arithmetic performed at the wrong width.

inputs.name: integer_u8_high_bit
inputs.description:
This is 128, the smallest u8 value with the high bit set and the first value
that cannot be represented as i8. Reinterpreting the same byte as i8 produces
-128, while narrowing or checked conversion may reject it. The value also
exposes accidental sign extension and signed comparisons in byte-oriented
code.

inputs.name: integer_u8_maximum
inputs.description:
This is the maximum u8 value, 255, with every bit set. Adding 1 crosses the
unsigned upper boundary and may trap, wrap to zero, saturate, or be widened
before evaluation. Reinterpreting the same byte as i8 produces -1, making it
useful for detecting signedness mistakes in storage, comparison, and
conversion code.

inputs.name: integer_u8_minimum
inputs.description:
This is the minimum u8 value, 0. Subtracting 1 crosses the unsigned lower
boundary and may trap, wrap to 255, saturate, or be widened before evaluation.
It is also an invalid divisor for integer division and remainder, making the
pair with one useful for both underflow and divide-by-zero handling.

inputs.name: integer_u8_one
inputs.description:
One is a baseline u8 operand for boundary arithmetic. Adding it to the maximum
overflows, subtracting it from zero underflows, and dividing it by zero
exercises integer divide-by-zero handling. It also provides a simple value for
detecting accidental signed decoding.

inputs.name: integer_u8_signed_maximum
inputs.description:
This is 127, the greatest u8 value that is also representable as i8. Adding 1
sets the high bit and reaches 128, the first u8 value outside the i8 positive
range. The adjacent pair targets off-by-one range checks, narrowing
conversions, and code that mistakes the high bit for a sign bit.

inputs.name: invalid_f32_truncated
inputs.description:
The first three bytes of the little-endian f32 encoding of 1.0
(00 00 80 3f) with the final byte missing. An f32 requires exactly four
bytes, so any decoder that reads a fixed-width float from this payload must
fail rather than read past the end or zero-fill the missing byte. It is
stored as raw bytes because it is not a well-formed f32.

inputs.name: invalid_utf16_lone_high_surrogate
inputs.description:
An ASCII 'A' (0x0041) followed by a lone high surrogate (0xD800) in
little-endian UTF-16. A high surrogate must be followed by a low surrogate in
the range 0xDC00..=0xDFFF; on its own it is not a valid Unicode scalar value
and well-formed UTF-16 decoders must reject it. It is stored as raw bytes
because it cannot be encoded as valid UTF-16.

See the Unicode UTF-16 conformance rules (D91, well-formed UTF-16):
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_above_unicode_maximum
inputs.description:
F4 90 80 80 would represent U+110000, one code point above Unicode's maximum
U+10FFFF. For an 0xF4 starter the second byte may be at most 0x8F; this input
tests the exact upper-bound check without using an otherwise malformed byte.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_cesu8_surrogate_pair
inputs.description:
This is the CESU-8 representation of GRINNING FACE (U+1F600): its UTF-16
surrogate pair is encoded as two three-byte sequences. Standard UTF-8 must
encode U+1F600 directly as F0 9F 98 80 and must reject both surrogate
sequences. This catches accidental CESU-8 or Java Modified UTF-8 acceptance.

See Unicode Technical Report #26 on CESU-8:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr26/

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_chunk_above_maximum_prefix
inputs.description:
This chunk starts a four-byte sequence whose second byte already places the
result above U+10FFFF. Appending the paired two-continuation chunk produces
F4 90 80 80, or U+110000. The pair tests whether upper-bound validation is
preserved across input-buffer boundaries.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_chunk_overlong_nul_prefix
inputs.description:
This chunk contains only 0xC0, which is not a legal UTF-8 starter. When the
paired unexpected-continuation chunk is appended, the result is C0 80, the
invalid overlong representation of NUL. The pair tests validators that reset,
defer, or incorrectly relax checks at chunk boundaries.

See RFC 3629's shortest-form requirement:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3629

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_chunk_surrogate_prefix
inputs.description:
This chunk is an incomplete three-byte sequence for the surrogate range.
Appending the paired unexpected-continuation chunk produces ED A0 80, the
invalid direct encoding of high surrogate U+D800. The pair tests decoders
whose range checks differ when a sequence crosses input buffers.

See Unicode definition D92 and Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_chunk_two_continuations
inputs.description:
Two continuation bytes cannot form a UTF-8 string by themselves. Appending
this chunk to the paired F4 90 prefix produces F4 90 80 80, the invalid
encoding of U+110000. It exercises stream validators that classify chunks
independently or lose upper-bound state between buffers.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_continuation_between_ascii
inputs.description:
A lone continuation byte is surrounded by valid ASCII. This checks that
validation covers the whole input rather than accepting valid prefixes and
suffixes, and exposes differences in replacement and error-recovery behavior.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_encoded_high_surrogate
inputs.description:
ED A0 80 is the direct three-byte encoding of U+D800, the first high
surrogate. Surrogate code points are excluded from Unicode scalar values and
are not permitted in well-formed UTF-8, even though the byte pattern has the
general shape of a three-byte sequence.

See Unicode definition D92 and Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_extra_continuation
inputs.description:
C2 A2 is a complete encoding of CENT SIGN (U+00A2), followed by an extra
continuation byte. A decoder must not attach the third byte to the completed
character or ignore it after successfully decoding the prefix.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_forbidden_leading_byte_fe
inputs.description:
The byte 0xFE is never permitted in UTF-8. Older or overly permissive
decoders may mistake it for a sequence starter left over from obsolete
pre-RFC 3629 forms, while strict decoders must reject it immediately.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_non_continuation_fourth_byte
inputs.description:
The first three bytes begin a valid four-byte sequence, but ASCII LEFT
PARENTHESIS appears where the final continuation byte is required. It
exercises validation of the last byte and error offsets at the end of a
nearly complete supplementary-plane character.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_non_continuation_second_byte
inputs.description:
A three-byte starter is followed by ASCII LEFT PARENTHESIS instead of a
continuation byte. The trailing 0xA1 also cannot stand alone. This familiar
malformed sequence exposes disagreement about how many replacement
characters to emit and where decoding should resume.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_non_continuation_third_byte
inputs.description:
The first two bytes begin a valid three-byte sequence, but ASCII LEFT
PARENTHESIS appears where the final continuation byte is required. This
targets decoders that validate only the first continuation or consume the
following ASCII byte as though it completed the character.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_obsolete_five_byte_sequence
inputs.description:
This is shaped like the five-byte UTF-8 forms allowed by early historical
definitions. Modern UTF-8 is limited to one through four bytes and Unicode
scalar values no greater than U+10FFFF, so the 0xF8 starter is invalid.
This catches decoders that still implement the obsolete 31-bit form.

See RFC 3629, which restricts UTF-8 to four bytes:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3629

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_overlong_four_byte_boundary
inputs.description:
F0 8F BF BF would decode to U+FFFF, whose shortest UTF-8 form uses three
bytes. For an 0xF0 starter the second byte must be at least 0x90; this
just-below boundary value tests that special lower-bound check.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_overlong_nul
inputs.description:
C0 80 is the non-shortest, two-byte representation historically used for
U+0000 by Modified UTF-8. Standard UTF-8 requires NUL to be encoded as the
single byte 00 and forbids both the 0xC0 starter and overlong encodings.
Accepting this form can reintroduce NUL after validation.

See RFC 3629's shortest-form requirement:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3629

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_overlong_three_byte_boundary
inputs.description:
E0 9F BF would decode to U+07FF, whose shortest UTF-8 form uses two bytes.
For an 0xE0 starter the second byte must be at least 0xA0; this just-below
boundary value tests that special lower-bound check.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_truncated_four_byte_sequence
inputs.description:
These are the first three bytes of the UTF-8 encoding of PILE OF POO
(U+1F4A9); the final continuation byte is missing. It targets decoders that
mishandle end-of-buffer state for four-byte supplementary-plane characters.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_truncated_three_byte_sequence
inputs.description:
These are the first two bytes of the UTF-8 encoding of EURO SIGN (U+20AC);
the final continuation byte is missing. The paired ASCII chunk is valid by
itself, but concatenating it after this prefix produces E2 82 41, which is
still invalid because 0x41 cannot complete the sequence.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_truncated_two_byte_sequence
inputs.description:
The 0xC2 starter requires one continuation byte, but the input ends
immediately. Streaming and buffer-oriented decoders must report an incomplete
sequence rather than reading beyond the buffer or accepting a partial value.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: invalid_utf8_unexpected_continuation
inputs.description:
A continuation byte appears without a preceding multi-byte sequence starter.
Decoders must reject it rather than treating it as a standalone character,
skipping it, or silently replacing it.

This input is also the right-hand chunk for paired boundary cases. Appending
it to the paired prefixes produces an overlong NUL or a surrogate encoding;
appending it to the paired valid cent sign produces an extra continuation.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: language_html_data_active_close_script_throw
inputs.description:
Inside an inline script this closes the script at the HTML tokenizer layer,
inserts an SVG load handler that throws the standard canary error, and opens a
replacement script element. JavaScript string escaping alone does not prevent
this HTML context break.

The thrown error is the only intended runtime side effect.

inputs.name: language_html_data_active_double_quoted_attribute_throw
inputs.description:
When interpolated inside a double-quoted attribute, the leading quote and
greater-than close the original context, an SVG load handler throws the
recognizable canary error, and the trailing fragment reopens an attribute to
reduce unrelated parse noise.

Correct attribute encoding keeps the entire value inert. The active branch
performs no network or persistent side effects.

inputs.name: language_html_data_active_element_load_throw
inputs.description:
If interpreted as HTML, this SVG load handler throws an uncaught Error with a
stable message. Browser test runners can detect it through page-error or
window.onerror hooks. The canary performs no network access, storage changes,
dialogs, or application calls.

This is an active counterpart to the inert custom-element canary and should
only be used where executing a local error handler is acceptable.

inputs.name: language_html_data_active_event_handler_body_throw
inputs.description:
If application data is used directly as an inline event-handler body, this
throws the standard canary Error when that event fires. It is useful for test
harnesses that can trigger the target event and observe uncaught page errors.

The value performs no action before the surrounding event is dispatched.

inputs.name: language_html_data_active_javascript_url_throw
inputs.description:
When navigated as a JavaScript URL, this throws the standard canary Error.
Test harnesses can activate the URL and watch for a page error without making
a network request, changing storage, displaying a dialog, or calling
application functions.

Attribute encoding does not make the scheme safe; URL scheme validation is
still required.

inputs.name: language_html_data_active_single_quoted_attribute_throw
inputs.description:
This is the single-quoted-attribute counterpart to the double-quoted breakout.
Unsafe interpolation creates an SVG whose load handler throws the stable
canary error; correct context-aware encoding preserves the bytes as data.

The handler does not access the network, DOM application state, or storage.

inputs.name: language_html_data_active_srcdoc_throw
inputs.description:
If assigned to an iframe srcdoc sink, this self-contained document throws the
standard canary Error during parsing. It requires no external resource and
provides a strong signal that supposedly inert application data reached an
active HTML-document context.

The script performs no action other than throwing.

inputs.name: language_html_data_active_unquoted_attribute_throw
inputs.description:
In an unquoted attribute value, this closes the current tag and inserts an SVG
load handler that throws the standard canary error. It gives automated browser
tests a high-signal failure for templates that omit attribute quotes.

Correct quoting or DOM property assignment keeps it inert.

inputs.name: language_html_data_ampersand
inputs.description:
Ampersand begins an HTML character reference. Incorrect or repeated encoding
can consume following characters, change visible text, or turn an encoded
delimiter back into syntax-significant data.

inputs.name: language_html_data_attribute_backtick
inputs.description:
Backtick is not an HTML quote delimiter, but it is syntax-significant when an
attribute is embedded in a JavaScript template literal or processed by legacy
templating and browser code. It catches context confusion between layers.

inputs.name: language_html_data_attribute_double_quote
inputs.description:
Double quote terminates a double-quoted HTML attribute value. Attribute
encoding must handle it separately from HTML text encoding or later bytes can
become new attributes or markup.

HTML defines separate tokenizer states for quoted and unquoted attributes:
https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/parsing.html#attribute-value-(double-quoted)-state

inputs.name: language_html_data_attribute_entity_double_quote
inputs.description:
The named character reference becomes a quote character in the DOM attribute
value but does not terminate the source attribute during one parse. Repeated
decoding or serializing and reparsing the value can change that safety
property.

inputs.name: language_html_data_attribute_event_handler_name
inputs.description:
The value is an event-handler attribute name. Applications that accept dynamic
attribute names cannot make them safe with ordinary value encoding and should
reject event handlers through an allowlist.

inputs.name: language_html_data_attribute_quoted_breakout
inputs.description:
When placed unescaped inside a double-quoted attribute, the first byte closes
the value and the rest adds a benign data-xss-canary attribute. Correct
attribute encoding keeps the whole value inside the original attribute.

inputs.name: language_html_data_attribute_single_quote
inputs.description:
Apostrophe terminates a single-quoted HTML attribute value. Escaping only
double quotes leaves templates that use single-quoted attributes vulnerable
to context changes.

inputs.name: language_html_data_attribute_srcdoc_name
inputs.description:
The iframe srcdoc attribute interprets its value as an HTML document. Dynamic
attribute-name systems should treat it as a distinct active-content sink, not
as an ordinary text-valued attribute.

inputs.name: language_html_data_attribute_style_name
inputs.description:
The style attribute switches its value into CSS parsing. Applications that
allow user-selected attribute names must account for this context transition
rather than applying only HTML attribute escaping.

inputs.name: language_html_data_attribute_unquoted_space_breakout
inputs.description:
In an unquoted attribute value, ASCII space ends the value and the remaining
bytes create a harmless data-xss-canary attribute. Quoted attributes or DOM
property assignment preserve the complete string as data.

inputs.name: language_html_data_attribute_unquoted_tag_breakout
inputs.description:
In an unquoted attribute this value closes the current tag and introduces an
inert custom element. It is a non-executing canary for templates that omit
quotes or rely on validation instead of output encoding.

inputs.name: language_html_data_close_script_canary
inputs.description:
The HTML tokenizer recognizes a script end tag before JavaScript string
parsing. Unescaped data inside an inline script can therefore close the script
and create this inert custom element even when JavaScript quoting appears
correct.

Script-data tokenization is defined here:
https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/parsing.html#script-data-state

inputs.name: language_html_data_close_style_canary
inputs.description:
The HTML parser recognizes a style end tag independently of CSS tokenization.
Raw data inside a style element can close it and create this inert custom
element even if the data was escaped only for CSS.

inputs.name: language_html_data_close_textarea_canary
inputs.description:
In textarea RCDATA, this value closes the surrounding element and then adds
an inert custom element. Generic HTML escaping or plain text setters prevent
the context break; raw template interpolation changes the DOM structure.

inputs.name: language_html_data_close_title_canary
inputs.description:
In title RCDATA, this value closes the title and introduces an inert custom
element. It catches renderers that correctly escape ordinary body text but
reuse the same unescaped interpolation path inside document metadata.

inputs.name: language_html_data_comment_closer
inputs.description:
A comment closer can terminate a surrounding HTML comment and expose later
bytes as markup. It also catches sanitizers that remove comment-looking user
data rather than encoding it for the destination context.

inputs.name: language_html_data_comment_opener
inputs.description:
A comment opener can hide following markup when interpolated into HTML source
and can leave the parser in comment state until later data supplies a closer.
Correct text-context encoding preserves the four characters.

inputs.name: language_html_data_double_encoded_lt
inputs.description:
One HTML decode produces the visible text "<", while a second produces
less-than. This catches applications where storage, templates, client code,
and DOM APIs each perform an encoding or decoding pass.

inputs.name: language_html_data_embedded_nul
inputs.description:
NUL separates an ASCII prefix from an inert element canary. C-string
boundaries can truncate before the markup, while the HTML input preprocessing
rules replace U+0000 with U+FFFD, causing validation, logging, and browser
parsing to operate on different values.

HTML input-stream preprocessing covers NULL handling here:
https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/parsing.html#preprocessing-the-input-stream

inputs.name: language_html_data_fullwidth_angle_element
inputs.description:
Fullwidth angle brackets are not HTML delimiters, but NFKC normalization maps
them to ASCII less-than and greater-than. Normalizing after validation and
before HTML insertion can therefore turn this inert-looking text into the
paired custom-element markup.

inputs.name: language_html_data_greater_than
inputs.description:
Greater-than can close a start tag, end tag, declaration, or malformed token
created by adjacent application data. It is harmless text when encoded for the
correct output context.

inputs.name: language_html_data_harmless_element
inputs.description:
This inert custom-element tag is a visible canary for raw HTML interpretation.
Correct text insertion displays the angle brackets; unsafe innerHTML or
template interpolation creates an x-xss-canary DOM element without executing
script or loading a resource.

inputs.name: language_html_data_invalid_utf8
inputs.description:
A lone 0xff byte appears before an inert element canary. Strict UTF-8
decoders, replacement decoders, legacy encodings, filters, and browsers may
disagree about the resulting character stream and delimiter positions.

inputs.name: language_html_data_less_than
inputs.description:
Less-than begins HTML markup in text contexts. A correctly escaped value
should remain visible text; raw interpolation can instead enter the tag-open
tokenizer state or combine with surrounding data to create an element.

The HTML tokenizer states are defined here:
https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/parsing.html#tokenization

inputs.name: language_html_data_named_lt_entity
inputs.description:
This source text decodes to a literal less-than character in the DOM. It does
not create a tag during a single HTML parse, but a second decode or an
encode-then-decode pipeline can turn it into raw markup before insertion.

inputs.name: language_html_data_numeric_lt_entity
inputs.description:
A hexadecimal numeric character reference decodes to less-than. Filters that
recognize only named entities or that decode before validating may disagree
with the browser about the final character stream.

inputs.name: language_html_data_script_backslash
inputs.description:
Backslash changes the meaning of the following JavaScript source character.
Manual escaping that processes quotes before backslashes can be bypassed or
can create an unterminated string.

inputs.name: language_html_data_script_backtick
inputs.description:
Backtick terminates a JavaScript template literal. Code that safely handles
single- and double-quoted strings can still break when a template literal is
introduced during refactoring.

inputs.name: language_html_data_script_double_quote
inputs.description:
Double quote terminates a double-quoted JavaScript string. This catches
renderers that select HTML attribute or text encoding despite emitting data
into JavaScript source.

inputs.name: language_html_data_script_line_separator
inputs.description:
U+2028 LINE SEPARATOR has historically differed between JSON, JavaScript
string literals, minifiers, and line-oriented tooling. It can split generated
source or produce a syntax error in older parsers if not serialized safely.

inputs.name: language_html_data_script_paragraph_separator
inputs.description:
U+2029 PARAGRAPH SEPARATOR stresses the same generated-source boundary as
U+2028, including legacy JavaScript parsers, JSON-to-script embedding,
minification, and error-location reporting.

inputs.name: language_html_data_script_single_quote
inputs.description:
Apostrophe terminates a single-quoted JavaScript string. HTML escaping alone
is not sufficient for data embedded in script source; a JavaScript-aware
serializer or separate data channel is required.

inputs.name: language_html_data_script_template_expression
inputs.description:
This sequence begins a JavaScript template substitution. If copied directly
into a template literal it attempts to resolve the deliberately undefined
xssCanary identifier and should throw, while safe serialization preserves the
characters as data.

inputs.name: language_html_data_small_angle_element
inputs.description:
U+FE64 SMALL LESS-THAN SIGN and U+FE65 SMALL GREATER-THAN SIGN normalize to
ASCII angle brackets under NFKC. This catches filters whose normalization
order differs from the renderer or storage layer.

inputs.name: language_html_data_style_closing_parenthesis
inputs.description:
Closing parenthesis can terminate a CSS function such as url(), calc(), or
var(). Data inserted into a declaration needs syntax-aware handling rather
than generic HTML or JavaScript escaping.

inputs.name: language_html_data_style_comment_closer
inputs.description:
Star-slash closes a surrounding CSS comment. Sanitizers that wrap untrusted
data in comments or strip comment-looking text can expose following bytes to
the CSS parser.

inputs.name: language_html_data_style_semicolon
inputs.description:
Semicolon ends a CSS declaration and can make later bytes begin another
property. It is a compact canary for values interpolated directly into style
attributes or style elements.

inputs.name: language_html_data_url_data_html_canary
inputs.description:
This data URL contains only an inert custom element, but navigation or iframe
sinks will parse it as a new HTML document. URL allowlists should distinguish
data-bearing schemes from ordinary network URLs.

inputs.name: language_html_data_url_entity_javascript_scheme
inputs.description:
HTML character-reference decoding changes the source text into the JavaScript
scheme before URL parsing. A filter that checks the encoded bytes but inserts
them into an attribute can therefore see a different scheme from the browser.

inputs.name: language_html_data_url_javascript_scheme
inputs.description:
The value is the JavaScript URL scheme with no script body. URL-valued sinks
require scheme allowlisting in addition to HTML attribute encoding; escaping
quotes does not make an active scheme safe.

The URL parser's scheme handling is defined here:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#scheme-state

inputs.name: language_html_data_url_protocol_relative
inputs.description:
A leading double slash is a scheme-relative network URL, not an application
path. Code that permits values beginning with slash but checks only the first
character can unintentionally navigate to another origin.

inputs.name: language_html_data_url_tabbed_javascript_scheme
inputs.description:
An ASCII tab splits the visible spelling of the JavaScript scheme. The URL
parsing algorithm removes ASCII tab and newline characters from input, so
validators and browsers can disagree if validation occurs before canonical
URL parsing.

inputs.name: language_html_data_url_userinfo_host_confusion
inputs.description:
The trusted-looking hostname appears in the userinfo portion; the actual host
is example.invalid after the at sign. String-prefix checks and human review can
therefore disagree with the parsed URL.

inputs.name: language_html_data_utf8_bom_element
inputs.description:
U+FEFF precedes an inert element canary. Layers may strip it as a byte-order
mark only at the start of a buffer, preserve it as application data, or move
the value between positions where its treatment changes.

inputs.name: language_html_data_zero_width_space_javascript
inputs.description:
U+200B ZERO WIDTH SPACE visually splits the JavaScript scheme without obvious
width. Browsers do not generally remove it as URL whitespace, but filters,
search tools, and custom normalizers may, creating a dangerous validation and
canonicalization differential.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_above_u64_max
inputs.description:
This decimal integer is one above the unsigned 64-bit maximum. It stresses
numeric validation and database bindings that disagree about unsigned support,
arbitrary precision, or overflow reporting.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_apostrophe_name
inputs.description:
A realistic name containing an apostrophe catches unsafe interpolation and
overzealous rejection without looking like a synthetic attack string.
Correct parameter binding should preserve the exact eight-character value.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_backslash
inputs.description:
Backslash handling varies by SQL dialect and server configuration. A bound
value should remain one backslash, while manual escaping can consume it,
duplicate it, or turn following characters into escapes.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_below_i64_min
inputs.description:
This decimal integer is one below the signed 64-bit minimum. Binding or
converting it to BIGINT should fail cleanly rather than wrap, clamp, or pass
through a narrower host-language integer first.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_block_comment_closer
inputs.description:
Star-slash can unexpectedly close a surrounding SQL comment or confuse a
comment-stripping layer. Correct value binding stores it verbatim.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_block_comment_opener
inputs.description:
Slash-star can open an SQL block comment if a value escapes its intended
literal or identifier context. It also catches sanitizers that strip
comment-looking text from legitimate data.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_crlf
inputs.description:
An embedded CRLF pair stresses newline normalization, SQL logging, and comment
handling across Windows and Unix boundaries. Both control bytes must survive
if the database value is intended to preserve exact text.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_curly_apostrophe_name
inputs.description:
U+2019 RIGHT SINGLE QUOTATION MARK resembles an ASCII apostrophe but is not an
SQL quote delimiter. Smart-quote conversion or normalization performed after
validation can turn safe data into syntax-significant data.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_current_timestamp_keyword
inputs.description:
CURRENT_TIMESTAMP can be evaluated by SQL when interpolated as a token rather
than stored as user text. This detects missing value quoting and accidental
expression injection without containing a destructive operation.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_default_keyword
inputs.description:
DEFAULT has special meaning in INSERT and UPDATE syntax. Unsafe unquoted
interpolation can substitute a column default instead of storing the literal
word, while parameter binding should preserve the text.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_double_quote
inputs.description:
A double quote stresses code that incorrectly uses identifier quoting for
values, or targets a dialect or mode where double quotes can delimit strings.
Bound string data should preserve it unchanged.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_doubled_single_quote
inputs.description:
Two apostrophes are SQL's standard in-literal representation of one
apostrophe. Treating already-escaped application data as raw data can double
escape it again or change the stored value.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_embedded_nul
inputs.description:
NUL between printable prefixes can truncate C-string database APIs, logs, or
escaping routines while length-aware layers retain the suffix. The complete
value is the thirteen bytes "prefix", NUL, and "suffix".

inputs.name: language_sql_data_empty
inputs.description:
A zero-byte value distinguishes an empty string from SQL NULL and exposes
