bindings, validators, or serializers that reject or silently replace empty
application data.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_fullwidth_apostrophe_name
inputs.description:
U+FF07 FULLWIDTH APOSTROPHE normalizes to ASCII apostrophe under NFKC. Systems
that normalize after escaping or validation can introduce an SQL delimiter
that was not present in the checked input.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_hex_looking
inputs.description:
The text resembles a hexadecimal SQL literal, but string-to-number conversion
rules differ from source-code literal parsing across engines. Binding it as
text, integer, or unquoted SQL can yield different values or errors.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_huge_exponent
inputs.description:
A small significand with an extreme exponent can overflow floating-point
bindings, become infinity, or be rejected by exact decimal types. It also
catches validators that cap digit count but ignore exponent magnitude.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_identifier_backtick
inputs.description:
Backtick is an identifier delimiter in MySQL and SQLite compatibility syntax
but ordinary content elsewhere. A dynamic identifier containing it exposes
dialect assumptions and incomplete delimiter doubling.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_identifier_close_bracket
inputs.description:
Closing bracket can terminate a bracket-quoted identifier in SQL Server and
SQLite. It should be rejected or escaped by code that supports user-selected
identifiers, never copied directly into SQL source.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_identifier_comma_list
inputs.description:
A comma can turn one requested identifier into a list of two SQL expressions.
This catches dynamic SELECT and ORDER BY code that validates characters but
permits structure-changing punctuation.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_identifier_double_quote
inputs.description:
An embedded double quote can terminate a standard delimited identifier unless
it is doubled correctly. It tests dynamic identifier quoting separately from
ordinary string-value escaping.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_identifier_qualified_name
inputs.description:
A period can turn one dynamic identifier into a schema-qualified name or be
treated as literal identifier content when quoted. Code that quotes the whole
value and code that quotes each path component produce different SQL.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_identifier_reserved_word
inputs.description:
The value is a common reserved SQL keyword. Applications that insert user
supplied table, column, alias, or sort names cannot use value parameters and
must apply dialect-correct identifier allowlisting and quoting.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_identifier_wildcard
inputs.description:
Asterisk can expand to every selected column when copied as SQL syntax, while
a quoted identifier named "*" has very different meaning. Dynamic projection
and ordering APIs should reject this value unless expansion is intentional.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_infinity
inputs.description:
Infinity may be accepted by floating-point or unconstrained numeric columns,
rejected by exact types, or preserved only as text. It tests conversion errors
and non-finite values crossing application and database boundaries.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_invalid_utf8
inputs.description:
The value contains a lone 0xff byte between ASCII prefixes. Text-mode drivers
should reject it predictably, while binary bindings should preserve it without
replacement, truncation, or disagreement about error offsets.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_leading_trailing_whitespace
inputs.description:
One leading and one trailing ASCII space catch automatic trimming in forms,
validation, binding, fixed-width columns, and result comparison. The intended
value is seven bytes, not the five-letter word alone.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_like_escaped_percent
inputs.description:
Backslash-percent is treated as a literal percent only when the query's LIKE
escape convention agrees. Dialects and explicit ESCAPE clauses differ, so
pre-escaping data can store or match the wrong bytes.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_like_percent
inputs.description:
Percent matches any sequence in an SQL LIKE pattern. Applications that intend
a literal substring search must escape it separately from ordinary SQL string
quoting or risk unexpectedly broad matches.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_like_percent_underscore
inputs.description:
Combined LIKE wildcards match any non-empty string in common SQL dialects.
This is a compact canary for search APIs that quote SQL safely but forget that
LIKE has its own wildcard language.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_like_underscore
inputs.description:
Underscore matches one character in an SQL LIKE pattern. Treating user input
as a literal search term without pattern escaping can therefore match values
the user did not request.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_line_comment_marker
inputs.description:
Two hyphens can begin an SQL line comment after unsafe interpolation, hiding
closing syntax or later predicates. As a parameter value they must remain two
ordinary characters.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_nan
inputs.description:
NaN is accepted by some SQL floating and numeric types and rejected or stored
as text by others. Equality, ordering, uniqueness, and round-trip behavior can
also differ from host-language IEEE expectations.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_negative_zero
inputs.description:
Negative zero may be preserved by floating-point bindings but normalized by
integer, decimal, JSON, or SQL layers. Round trips can therefore change its
text or sign-sensitive behavior.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_newline
inputs.description:
An embedded line feed can break line-oriented SQL logging, change the reach of
a comment after interpolation, or be normalized during transport. The value
should remain one string containing two lines.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_null_keyword
inputs.description:
The four-character string NULL can become the SQL null value if inserted
without quotes. This catches code that confuses textual sentinel values with
bound null parameters.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_quote_semicolon_comment
inputs.description:
This compact, non-destructive canary combines a string terminator, statement
separator, and comment marker without adding an executable command. Unsafe
interpolation should fail visibly; parameter binding should store four bytes.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_semicolon
inputs.description:
A semicolon is harmless string data when bound correctly but can become a
statement separator when interpolated or passed through a naive SQL splitter.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_single_quote
inputs.description:
A lone apostrophe terminates a naively interpolated SQL string literal. It
should survive parameter binding as ordinary data and is the smallest useful
canary for missing or incorrect SQL string escaping.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_trailing_non_breaking_space
inputs.description:
The value ends with U+00A0 NO-BREAK SPACE rather than ASCII space. Form
trimming, SQL collations, fixed-width columns, and application comparisons may
disagree about whether it is significant.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_utf8_bom
inputs.description:
U+FEFF appears at the start of an otherwise ordinary value. Some decoders
strip it as a byte-order mark even when it is application data, causing stored
and queried values to differ.

inputs.name: language_sql_data_zero_width_space_keyword
inputs.description:
U+200B ZERO WIDTH SPACE splits the visible word UNION without adding visible
width. Filters, logs, and normalizers may disagree about whether it is present,
especially if validation and SQL construction occur in different layers.

inputs.name: line_break_ascii_lf
inputs.description:
This baseline separates "A" and "B" with ASCII LINE FEED (U+000A). It pairs
with Unicode line-boundary characters that may render similarly but are not
recognized by APIs, regular expressions, protocols, or file readers that
only handle LF or CRLF.

Unicode Standard Annex #14 defines line-break classes:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr14/

inputs.name: line_break_line_separator
inputs.description:
This input separates "A" and "B" with LINE SEPARATOR (U+2028). It can render
like an ordinary newline while remaining a distinct three-byte UTF-8
sequence, exposing inconsistent line counts, trimming, parsing, and logging.

Unicode Standard Annex #14 assigns U+2028 a mandatory break:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr14/

inputs.name: line_break_next_line
inputs.description:
This input separates "A" and "B" with NEXT LINE (U+0085). It is a Unicode
whitespace and line-boundary character encoded as two UTF-8 bytes, but many
line-oriented APIs recognize only ASCII LF or CRLF.

Unicode Standard Annex #14 assigns U+0085 the Next Line line-break class:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr14/

inputs.name: line_break_paragraph_separator
inputs.description:
This input separates "A" and "B" with PARAGRAPH SEPARATOR (U+2029). It can
display as a line or paragraph break while remaining a distinct three-byte
UTF-8 sequence, testing parser agreement, logging, trimming, and line counts.

Unicode Standard Annex #14 assigns U+2029 a mandatory break:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr14/

inputs.name: noncharacter_10ffff
inputs.description:
This input is the single noncharacter U+10FFFF, the highest Unicode scalar
value. It is validly encoded as four-byte UTF-8 and exercises inclusive
upper-bound checks, scalar-value handling, noncharacter policy, and systems
that accidentally reject the legal endpoint of Unicode's code space.

The Unicode Consortium FAQ explains plane-ending noncharacters:
https://www.unicode.org/faq/private_use.html#nonchar1

inputs.name: noncharacter_1fffe
inputs.description:
This input is the single supplementary-plane noncharacter U+1FFFE. Its
well-formed UTF-8 encoding requires four bytes, exercising code paths that
combine noncharacter policy with supplementary-plane decoding and length
calculations.

The Unicode Consortium FAQ explains plane-ending noncharacters:
https://www.unicode.org/faq/private_use.html#nonchar1

inputs.name: noncharacter_fdd0
inputs.description:
This input is the single noncharacter U+FDD0. It is a valid Unicode scalar
value with a well-formed UTF-8 encoding, but is permanently reserved rather
than assigned to an abstract character. It tests the distinction between
UTF-8 validity, character assignment, and application-level text policy.

The Unicode Consortium FAQ explains noncharacters:
https://www.unicode.org/faq/private_use.html#nonchar1

inputs.name: noncharacter_fffe
inputs.description:
This input is the single noncharacter U+FFFE. Its UTF-8 encoding is
well-formed even though the code point is permanently reserved. It is useful
for catching decoders or validators that incorrectly equate noncharacter
status with malformed UTF-8.

The Unicode Consortium FAQ explains noncharacters:
https://www.unicode.org/faq/private_use.html#nonchar1

inputs.name: noncharacter_ffff
inputs.description:
This input is the single noncharacter U+FFFF, the final code point in the
Basic Multilingual Plane. It has a valid three-byte UTF-8 encoding and tests
code that confuses plane boundaries, sentinel conventions, or unassigned
characters with ill-formed text.

The Unicode Consortium FAQ explains noncharacters:
https://www.unicode.org/faq/private_use.html#nonchar1

inputs.name: real_world_email_all_atext_symbols
inputs.description:
This local-part contains every non-alphanumeric ASCII symbol allowed by the
RFC 5322 `atext` production. Validators built around a small
letters-digits-dot-hyphen character class often reject one or more of them,
even though the unquoted dot-string is valid SMTP mailbox syntax.

RFC 5322 section 3.2.3 defines `atext`, and RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 imports it:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322.html#section-3.2.3
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_email_apostrophe_local_part
inputs.description:
Apostrophe is an ordinary `atext` character and needs no quoting in an SMTP
local-part. This realistic name-shaped address catches validators that allow
only word characters and punctuation handling that assumes apostrophe always
delimits another language's string literal.

RFC 5322 section 3.2.3 includes apostrophe in `atext`, which RFC 5321 section
4.1.2 uses for SMTP atoms:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322.html#section-3.2.3
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_email_confusable_cyrillic
inputs.description:
The local-part uses Cyrillic U+0440, U+0430, U+0443, U+0440, and U+0430
followed by Latin `l`. RFC 6531 permits the non-ASCII atext under SMTPUTF8,
but the address remains unequal to the visually similar ASCII pair.

RFC 6531 section 3.3 extends atext with UTF-8 non-ASCII characters, and RFC
6530 section 13 discusses local-part homograph attacks:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6531.html#section-3.3
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6530.html#section-13

inputs.name: real_world_email_confusable_latin
inputs.description:
This all-ASCII address is the baseline for a visually similar SMTPUTF8
address whose local-part uses Cyrillic letters. They are distinct mailbox
names even when a font makes them difficult to distinguish.

RFC 6530 section 13 identifies homograph attacks in internationalized email:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6530.html#section-13

inputs.name: real_world_email_domain_lowercase
inputs.description:
This is the lowercase baseline for a domain-case pair. Mailbox domains follow
DNS comparison rules and are case-insensitive, so the paired address denotes
the same mailbox even though naive byte comparison says otherwise.

RFC 5321 section 2.3.11 states that mailbox domains are not case-sensitive:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-2.3.11

inputs.name: real_world_email_domain_uppercase
inputs.description:
The domain differs in ASCII case from the paired address but is equivalent
under DNS and SMTP mailbox-domain comparison. Systems that lowercase the
entire address go too far because the local-part has different rules.

RFC 5321 section 2.3.11 states that mailbox domains are not case-sensitive:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-2.3.11

inputs.name: real_world_email_header_comments_around_at
inputs.description:
This is a valid RFC 5322 message-header addr-spec: dot-atoms permit CFWS,
including comments, on either side of the at-sign. It is not RFC 5321 SMTP
envelope mailbox syntax. Treating all "email address" contexts as identical
either rejects a valid header or leaks comments into an envelope address.

RFC 5322 sections 3.2.3 and 3.4.1 define dot-atom CFWS and addr-spec:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322.html#section-3.2.3
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322.html#section-3.4.1

inputs.name: real_world_email_idn_a_label
inputs.description:
This ASCII-compatible domain is the A-label form of the paired Japanese
U-label domain. IDNA-aware comparison converts the U-label to A-label form
and applies normal DNS case-insensitive matching, so the two spellings are
equivalent.

RFC 5890 section 2.3.2.4 defines A-label/U-label equivalence:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5890.html#section-2.3.2.4

inputs.name: real_world_email_idn_u_label
inputs.description:
This uses the U-label presentation of the domain paired with its ASCII
A-label form. It is valid internationalized SMTP mailbox syntax when
SMTPUTF8 is available, but ASCII-only validators and transports may reject
it despite the equivalent domain representation.

RFC 6531 section 3.3 extends SMTP domains with U-labels, and RFC 5890 section
2.3.2.4 defines equivalence with A-labels:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6531.html#section-3.3
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5890.html#section-2.3.2.4

inputs.name: real_world_email_ipv4_address_literal
inputs.description:
SMTP permits an IPv4 address-literal in square brackets instead of a domain
name. This uses the documentation-only TEST-NET-1 range, so it tests syntax
without identifying a live host. Domain-only validators often reject it.

RFC 5321 sections 4.1.2 and 4.1.3 define address-literal syntax:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.3

inputs.name: real_world_email_ipv6_address_literal
inputs.description:
SMTP permits an IPv6 address-literal with a required `IPv6:` tag. The
address is from the documentation prefix. Parsers frequently reject the
brackets or mistake the colons for unrelated address syntax.

RFC 5321 sections 4.1.2 and 4.1.3 define IPv6-address-literal:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.3

inputs.name: real_world_email_local_part_lowercase
inputs.description:
This is paired with a local-part that differs only in case. SMTP requires
implementations to preserve and treat local-part case as significant, even
though exploiting that distinction is discouraged and many providers choose
case-insensitive local policy.

RFC 5321 section 2.3.11 defines local-part case sensitivity:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-2.3.11

inputs.name: real_world_email_local_part_nfc
inputs.description:
The local-part contains precomposed U+00E9 and looks the same as the paired
decomposed spelling. Both are syntactically valid SMTPUTF8 addresses, but
local-part interpretation belongs to the destination system; RFC 6530
recommends normalization or aliases rather than allowing intermediaries to
assume equivalence.

RFC 6530 section 10.1 discusses mailbox names and Unicode normalization:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6530.html#section-10.1

inputs.name: real_world_email_local_part_nfd
inputs.description:
The visible `é` in the local-part is U+0065 followed by U+0301. RFC 6530
explicitly says unnormalized local-part strings are valid but may not work
reliably, making this a trap for validators, account lookup, and systems that
normalize identifiers without the destination's authority.

RFC 6530 section 10.1 discusses valid unnormalized mailbox names:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6530.html#section-10.1

inputs.name: real_world_email_local_part_uppercase
inputs.description:
The local-part differs in case from the paired address. A generic
intermediary must not lowercase it: only the destination system can choose
a case-insensitive mailbox policy.

RFC 5321 section 2.3.11 requires local-part case to be preserved:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-2.3.11

inputs.name: real_world_email_maximum_local_part
inputs.description:
The local-part is exactly 64 ASCII octets, the maximum an SMTP
implementation must accept. Off-by-one checks, character-count assumptions,
and database columns sized for common addresses can reject or truncate it.

RFC 5321 section 4.5.3.1.1 sets the local-part limit to 64 octets:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.5.3.1.1

inputs.name: real_world_email_maximum_smtp_path
inputs.description:
This address is 254 ASCII octets: a 64-octet local-part, one at-sign, and a
189-octet domain. Enclosing it in the SMTP path angle brackets reaches the
256-octet forward-path limit exactly. It catches the common 255/256 off-by-one
error and storage schemas based on folklore rather than the transport limit.

RFC 5321 sections 4.5.3.1.1 through 4.5.3.1.3 define the local-part, domain,
and path limits:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.5.3.1.1
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.5.3.1.2
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.5.3.1.3

inputs.name: real_world_email_quoted_at_sign
inputs.description:
The at-sign is permitted as qtext inside a quoted local-part. Splitting on
the first at-sign, or requiring exactly one at-sign in the raw string, finds
the wrong local/domain boundary.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 permits ASCII 35 through 91 in qtextSMTP:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_email_quoted_consecutive_dots
inputs.description:
Consecutive dots are forbidden in a dot-string but are ordinary qtext inside
a quoted local-part. A validator that checks dot placement without first
parsing the quoted-string will incorrectly reject this mailbox.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines both Dot-string and Quoted-string:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_email_quoted_dot_string
inputs.description:
Quoting is unnecessary here, but the quoted local-part is equivalent to the
paired dot-string. Validators may reject it, while identity systems may
incorrectly create a second account for the alternate spelling.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 requires all quoted forms to be treated as equivalent:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_email_quoted_empty_local_part
inputs.description:
The quoted-string production permits zero qcontent characters, making an
empty quoted local-part syntactically valid. It is highly unlikely to be
provisioned and is commonly rejected by validators that require at least one
word character before the at-sign.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines `Quoted-string = DQUOTE *QcontentSMTP DQUOTE`:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_email_quoted_escaped_space
inputs.description:
The space is represented as a valid SMTP quoted-pair. The escape changes the
bytes of the address spelling but not the local-part it denotes, so raw
string comparison incorrectly treats this as distinct from its pair.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines quoted-pairSMTP and quoted-form equivalence:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_email_quoted_literal_space
inputs.description:
This quoted local-part contains an unescaped space and is the baseline for
the paired quoted-pair spelling. Both forms represent the same local-part;
the backslash in a quoted-pair is syntax rather than mailbox data.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 requires quoted-form equivalence, and RFC 5322 section
3.2.4 describes the semantic invisibility of quoted-pair backslashes:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322.html#section-3.2.4

inputs.name: real_world_email_quoted_single_space
inputs.description:
A space is explicitly allowed as qtextSMTP inside a quoted local-part, so
this one-space mailbox name is syntactically valid. Trimming, tokenization,
and validators that ban all whitespace can corrupt or reject it.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines qtextSMTP as including ASCII 32:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_email_slash_equals_local_part
inputs.description:
Slash and equals are both valid unquoted `atext` characters. This
routing-shaped local-part is syntactically valid but often fails simplified
regular expressions, URL-oriented escaping, and form-validation libraries.

RFC 5322 section 3.2.3 includes slash and equals in `atext`, which RFC 5321
section 4.1.2 uses for SMTP atoms:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322.html#section-3.2.3
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_email_unquoted_dot_string
inputs.description:
This minimally quoted dot-string is the baseline for the paired quoted
local-part. RFC 5321 requires all quoted forms of a local-part to compare
equivalently and recommends sending the form with the least quoting.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines quoted-form equivalence:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_bare_space_local_part
inputs.description:
Space is not `atext`, so it cannot appear bare in a dot-string local-part.
The paired quoted form is valid; removing or ignoring quotes makes these two
inputs dangerously easy to conflate.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines allowed Atom and Quoted-string content:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_consecutive_dots_local_part
inputs.description:
Consecutive dots create an empty atom and are invalid in an unquoted
local-part. The paired quoted local-part is valid, so parsers must apply dot
rules only after recognizing whether the local-part is quoted.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines both Dot-string and Quoted-string:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_crlf_header_injection
inputs.description:
A valid-looking addr-spec is followed by raw CRLF and an inert marker header.
It is not one email address. Code that interpolates an unchecked address into
message headers can turn the suffix into header structure.

RFC 5322 sections 2.2 and 3.4.1 define header field and addr-spec boundaries:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322.html#section-2.2
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322.html#section-3.4.1

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_domain_label_over_63_octets
inputs.description:
The first domain label contains 64 ASCII octets. It has otherwise ordinary
LDH characters, making it a useful boundary case for validators that check
total address length but not individual DNS labels.

RFC 1035 section 2.3.4 limits labels to 63 octets:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1035.html#section-2.3.4

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_empty_domain_label
inputs.description:
Consecutive dots create an empty domain label. Code that removes empty
elements after splitting can silently transform this into a different,
valid domain.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines Domain as dot-separated non-empty sub-domains:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_empty_unquoted_local_part
inputs.description:
The local-part before the at-sign is empty and therefore cannot match an SMTP
Dot-string or Quoted-string. The paired `""` local-part is valid, which
catches validators that discard quote syntax before checking emptiness.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 requires a non-empty Dot-string but permits an empty
Quoted-string:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_fake_idna_a_label
inputs.description:
`xn--a` has the reserved A-label prefix but is not a valid Punycode encoding
of a U-label. Code that recognizes only the prefix can accept a fake A-label
that IDNA-aware domain processing must reject.

RFC 5890 section 2.3.2.1 defines A-label validity and fake A-labels:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5890.html#section-2.3.2.1

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_invalid_utf8_local_part
inputs.description:
The local-part contains bytes C3 28, an invalid UTF-8 sequence. It cannot be
an ASCII SMTP mailbox or an SMTPUTF8 mailbox. Byte-oriented parsers that
replace malformed input may manufacture a different identifier.

RFC 6531 section 3.3 requires valid UTF-8 non-ASCII local-part content:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6531.html#section-3.3

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_ipv4_address_literal_overflow
inputs.description:
The first IPv4 component is 256 and cannot be an IPv4-address-literal. RFC
5322's generic domain-literal text grammar may accept the characters, so this
specifically checks SMTP semantic validation rather than bracket matching
alone.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.3 imports IPv4 syntax from RFC 4291:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.3

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_leading_dot_domain
inputs.description:
A domain cannot begin with a dot because the first sub-domain would be empty.
Splitting into labels while discarding empty strings can incorrectly repair
this into `example.com`.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines Domain as non-empty sub-domains separated by
dots:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_leading_dot_local_part
inputs.description:
An unquoted local-part cannot begin with a dot. A validator that merely
allows `atext` and dot characters without enforcing dot-atom structure may
accept this subtly malformed address.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines a Dot-string as atoms separated by dots:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_leading_hyphen_domain_label
inputs.description:
SMTP's preferred domain syntax permits hyphens only inside a label, not as
its first character. RFC 5322's generic dot-atom header grammar is looser,
so this specifically tests validators that claim to accept SMTP mailbox
domains.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines `sub-domain = Let-dig [Ldh-str]`:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_local_part_over_64_octets
inputs.description:
The local-part is 65 ASCII octets, one beyond the SMTP maximum. Character-
based limits and off-by-one comparisons frequently accept this boundary.

RFC 5321 section 4.5.3.1.1 limits local-parts to 64 octets:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.5.3.1.1

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_malformed_ipv6_address_literal
inputs.description:
The required `IPv6:` tag is present, but the address contains an illegal
triple colon. Character-class validation of hexadecimal digits and colons is
insufficient to recognize IPv6 structure.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.3 requires a syntactically valid IPv6 address:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.3

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_missing_at_sign
inputs.description:
This looks like a plausible domain-like identifier but has no at-sign and
therefore cannot be an addr-spec. Validators that only check for a dot or
domain suffix can accept it.

RFC 5322 section 3.4.1 requires `local-part "@" domain`:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322.html#section-3.4.1

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_missing_domain
inputs.description:
The at-sign is present but the required domain is empty. A parser that trims
delimiters or treats the at-sign as optional punctuation may return a local
mailbox name instead of rejecting the addr-spec.

RFC 5322 section 3.4.1 requires a domain after the at-sign:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5322.html#section-3.4.1

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_multiple_at_signs
inputs.description:
The first at-sign is unquoted, so the raw string cannot be one addr-spec.
Splitting only on the final at-sign can incorrectly reinterpret
`john@doe` as a local-part. The paired quoted spelling is valid.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 permits at-sign only inside a Quoted-string:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_nul_local_part
inputs.description:
A NUL byte appears inside the local-part. It is excluded from both atom and
quoted-string syntax, and passing the bytes through a C string API can
truncate the visible address before the at-sign.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 limits local-part characters:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_quote_inside_atom
inputs.description:
A quoted-string must constitute the complete local-part; a quote cannot
begin in the middle of an atom. Lenient parsers may discard the quote and
manufacture a different address.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 allows Local-part to be either Dot-string or
Quoted-string, not a mixture:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_smtp_path_over_maximum
inputs.description:
This address is 255 ASCII octets, so enclosing it in SMTP path angle
brackets requires 257 octets and exceeds the 256-octet forward-path limit by
one. The local-part and each domain label remain individually in range,
isolating the aggregate boundary.

RFC 5321 section 4.5.3.1.3 sets the path limit:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.5.3.1.3

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_trailing_dot_domain
inputs.description:
SMTP mailbox Domain syntax does not include the DNS presentation convention
of a final root dot. Stripping it may be a deployment policy, but the source
string is not an RFC 5321 mailbox.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 requires each dot to be followed by a sub-domain:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_trailing_dot_local_part
inputs.description:
An unquoted local-part cannot end with a dot because every dot in a
Dot-string must have a non-empty atom on both sides. Trimming punctuation can
accidentally turn this into a different, valid mailbox.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines Dot-string syntax:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_trailing_hyphen_domain_label
inputs.description:
An SMTP domain label must end in a letter or digit. A trailing hyphen often
slips through validators that use an unrestricted letters-digits-hyphens
character class.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 requires `Ldh-str` to end in Let-dig:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_unclosed_domain_literal
inputs.description:
The opening square bracket is never closed. A parser that scans backward for
punctuation or falls back from address-literal to ordinary domain parsing
may return a partial host instead of rejecting the mailbox.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 requires matching brackets around address literals:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_unclosed_quoted_local_part
inputs.description:
The opening quote is never closed, so the apparent at-sign remains inside an
unterminated quoted-string and there is no valid local/domain boundary.
Searching backward for an at-sign can misparse it.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 requires matching DQUOTE delimiters:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_email_unquoted_backslash_local_part
inputs.description:
Backslash is not an `atext` character and has escape semantics only inside a
quoted-string. Parsers that process escapes before identifying the quoting
context may silently accept or alter this mailbox.

RFC 5321 section 4.1.2 defines Atom and quoted-pairSMTP:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5321.html#section-4.1.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_backslash_scheme_separators
inputs.description:
Reverse solidus is not an RFC URI delimiter, but WHATWG special-URL parsing
treats these backslashes as slashes and repairs the input to
`https://example.com/path`. A validator and fetcher using different URL
dialects can therefore disagree on every component boundary.

RFC 3986 excludes raw backslash, while WHATWG special authority parsing
handles it as a validation-error separator:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-2
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#special-authority-ignore-slashes-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_data_after_ipv6_bracket
inputs.description:
Characters follow a complete bracketed IPv6 host without a colon introducing
a port. Ignoring the suffix would change the source authority and can make
allowlist display disagree with connection behavior.

The WHATWG host state accepts only a port or path delimiter after `]`:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#host-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_embedded_ipv4_overflow
inputs.description:
The IPv4 tail inside this IPv6 literal contains component 999. Parsers that
validate only the hexadecimal prefix can miss the invalid embedded address.

The WHATWG IPv6 parser invokes IPv4 parsing for dotted tails:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-ipv6-parser

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_empty_host_before_fragment
inputs.description:
The fragment delimiter arrives before any host characters. A parser that
stops structural validation at `#` might retain a hostless HTTPS URL instead
of failing the special-host state.

The WHATWG host state requires a non-empty host before `#`:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#host-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_empty_host_before_query
inputs.description:
The query delimiter terminates an empty HTTPS authority. Query parsing must
not begin until the required special host has been successfully parsed.

The WHATWG host state requires a non-empty host before `?`:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#host-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_empty_host_eof
inputs.description:
The HTTPS authority ends without a host. Although generic URI grammar can be
permissive about empty reg-name text, the WHATWG special-host parser must
fail because an HTTP-family URL requires a host.

The WHATWG host state rejects an empty special host:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#host-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_empty_punycode_a_label
inputs.description:
The host label consists only of the `xn--` ACE prefix and has no encoded
payload. Prefix checks alone can mistake it for an internationalized domain,
but domain-to-ASCII validation must fail.

RFC 5890 section 2.3.2.1 defines valid A-labels and fake A-labels:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5890.html#section-2.3.2.1

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_encoded_nul_host
inputs.description:
Host percent-decoding produces U+0000, a forbidden domain code point. C APIs
may truncate at the decoded NUL, making rejection essential before any host
comparison or DNS lookup.

The WHATWG host parser rejects forbidden domain code points:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#host-parsing
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#forbidden-domain-code-point

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_excess_authority_slashes
inputs.description:
Four slashes follow the HTTPS scheme instead of two. Browser-compatible
special-URL parsing ignores the extras and repairs this to
`https://example.com/path`; parsers following a different hierarchy model
can expose an empty authority and a path beginning with two slashes.

The WHATWG special authority ignore slashes state defines this repair:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#special-authority-ignore-slashes-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_fake_punycode_a_label
inputs.description:
`xn--a` has an A-label-shaped prefix but is not a valid Punycode encoding of
a U-label. WHATWG domain-to-ASCII processing must reject it rather than
treating it as an ordinary ASCII hostname.

RFC 5890 section 2.3.2.1 defines fake A-labels:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5890.html#section-2.3.2.1

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_file_url_port
inputs.description:
File URLs have no port. Reusing a generic network-URL authority parser can
retain `:80`, even though a conforming WHATWG file URL parser must reject it.

The WHATWG file host state does not permit a port:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#file-host-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_file_url_userinfo
inputs.description:
WHATWG file URLs cannot contain credentials. Generic authority parsers may
accept the userinfo and expose `host.example` as a host, while a conforming
file URL parser must fail.

The WHATWG authority state rejects credentials for file URLs:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#authority-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_idna_contextj_violation
inputs.description:
U+200D ZERO WIDTH JOINER occurs between Latin letters without the contextual
conditions required by IDNA. It is visually hidden, so deleting it can make
the invalid source appear to be the valid `ab.example` host.

RFC 5892 defines CONTEXTJ requirements for U+200D, which domain-to-ASCII
processing enforces:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5892.html#appendix-A.2

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_ipv4_component_overflow
inputs.description:
The first dotted IPv4 component is 256. The WHATWG parser recognizes the
host as an IPv4 candidate and must fail rather than falling back to an
ordinary domain name.

The WHATWG IPv4 parser checks the numeric address range:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-ipv4-parser

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_ipv4_too_many_parts
inputs.description:
An IPv4 address may contain at most four numeric parts. Generic domain code
may see five legal digit-only labels, but the WHATWG ends-in-a-number rule
selects IPv4 parsing and requires failure.

The WHATWG IPv4 parser limits the number of parts:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-ipv4-parser

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_malformed_ipv6_compression
inputs.description:
IPv6 permits at most one double-colon compression marker; a triple colon
cannot be parsed as a valid address. A character-class check for hex digits
and colons will miss this structural error.

The WHATWG IPv6 parser defines compression parsing:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-ipv6-parser

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_malformed_percent_host
inputs.description:
The percent sign in the host is not followed by two hexadecimal digits.
Unlike malformed percent text in a path, host parsing percent-decodes before
domain processing and this input must fail.

RFC 3986 section 2.1 defines percent-encoding, and the WHATWG host parser
applies percent-decoding:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-2.1
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#host-parsing

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_multiple_port_separators
inputs.description:
The second colon cannot occur in a non-bracketed host and port. Splitting on
the first or last colon produces different apparent port strings.

The WHATWG port state fails when it encounters the second colon:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#port-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_nonhex_percent_escape_path
inputs.description:
`%GG` has two following characters, but neither is hexadecimal. WHATWG
parsing can preserve it after a validation error, while decoders differ on
whether to reject, replace, or leave the percent sign literal.

RFC 3986 section 2.1 defines hexadecimal percent triplets:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-2.1
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#invalid-URL-unit

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_nonnumeric_port
inputs.description:
A special URL port may contain only ASCII digits. Treating an unparseable
port as absent would silently change the authority rather than rejecting the
input.

The WHATWG port state fails on non-digit port characters:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#port-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_raw_space_host
inputs.description:
ASCII space is forbidden inside a special URL host. Splitting on whitespace
or deleting it before parsing can manufacture the different host
`example.com`.

The WHATWG host parser rejects forbidden host code points:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#forbidden-host-code-point

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_scheme_starts_with_digit
inputs.description:
A URI scheme must begin with an ASCII letter. This cannot be parsed as an
absolute URL without a base, even though a loose `word:` pattern may accept
`1https` as a scheme.

RFC 3986 section 3.1 and the WHATWG scheme start state require an ASCII
letter:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-3.1
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#scheme-start-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_short_percent_escape_path
inputs.description:
`%2` is missing one hexadecimal digit and is not valid RFC percent-encoding.
WHATWG parsing records a validation error but preserves the malformed text in
the path, so strict validators and browser-compatible consumers disagree.

RFC 3986 section 2.1 requires exactly two hex digits, while the WHATWG parser
reports invalid-URL-unit without necessarily failing:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-2.1
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#invalid-URL-unit

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_signed_port
inputs.description:
A leading plus sign is not part of URL port syntax even though general
integer parsers often accept it. Reusing a permissive numeric conversion can
make this malformed authority appear valid.

The WHATWG port state accepts ASCII digits only:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#port-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_space_before_scheme_colon
inputs.description:
Space is not permitted between the scheme name and colon. Tokenizers that
trim each side of punctuation independently can silently repair this into a
different HTTPS URL.

RFC 3986 section 3.1 defines the scheme as immediately followed by `:`:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-3.1

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_tab_inside_host
inputs.description:
A raw tab splits the visible hostname bytes between `exa` and `mple.com`.
WHATWG preprocessing removes ASCII tabs anywhere in the input after recording
a validation error, repairing this to `example.com`; other parsers reject it
or retain the control character.

The WHATWG basic URL parser removes ASCII tab and newline characters:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-basic-url-parser

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_unclosed_ipv6_literal
inputs.description:
The opening bracket for the IPv6 literal is never closed. A parser must not
reinterpret the slash or end-of-input as an implicit closing bracket.

The WHATWG host state requires a matching `]` for bracketed hosts:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#host-state

inputs.name: real_world_invalid_url_userinfo_without_host
inputs.description:
Userinfo and its at-sign are present, but the required host is empty. Code
that validates only the username portion can leave a structurally incomplete
authority.

The WHATWG authority and host states reject an empty host:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#authority-state
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#host-state

inputs.name: real_world_url_apache_encoded_traversal
inputs.description:
This inert path uses `.%2e/`, the mixed literal/encoded parent-segment shape
associated with Apache HTTP Server's 2.4.49 path-normalization failure. The
destination is a documentation host and the leaf is a marker, not a
sensitive file.

Apache's advisories state that CVE-2021-41773 and its incomplete fix
CVE-2021-42013 allowed URLs to map outside Alias-like directories:
https://httpd.apache.org/security/vulnerabilities_24.html#CVE-2021-41773
https://httpd.apache.org/security/vulnerabilities_24.html#CVE-2021-42013

inputs.name: real_world_url_ascii_dot_host
inputs.description:
This ordinary ASCII full stop is the normalized baseline for a host using
U+3002 IDEOGRAPHIC FULL STOP. URL host processing maps the Unicode separator
to this dot before DNS lookup.

UTS #46 maps label-separator variants during domain processing:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr46/#Processing

inputs.name: real_world_url_backslash_authority_confusion
inputs.description:
For a WHATWG special URL, backslash is treated as a path separator, making
`trusted.example` the host and `@attacker.invalid` path data. RFC-oriented
parsers that do not treat backslash as a separator can instead interpret the
same suffix as authority data. Comparing this with the paired at-sign case
exposes parser-differential SSRF and allowlist bugs.

The WHATWG URL Standard defines backslash as a reverse solidus delimiter for
special URLs:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#special-scheme
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#url-parsing

inputs.name: real_world_url_backslash_traversal
inputs.description:
WHATWG special URLs treat backslash as a path separator, and Windows
filesystems do too. An RFC-only filter may see ordinary path data while a
browser, proxy, or filesystem resolves `..\` as a parent segment.

The WHATWG URL parser treats reverse solidus like slash for special schemes:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#url-path-state

inputs.name: real_world_url_confusable_cyrillic
inputs.description:
The apparent `paypal` label uses Cyrillic U+0440, U+0430, U+0443, U+0440,
U+0430 followed by Latin `l`. It serializes as
`xn--l-7sba6dbr.example` and is not the paired Latin host, despite their
visual similarity in many fonts.

UTS #39 documents confusable and mixed-script spoofing risks:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr39/

inputs.name: real_world_url_confusable_latin
inputs.description:
This all-Latin host is the baseline for a visually similar domain containing
Cyrillic letters. The pair is not equal: IDNA conversion preserves the
distinction and yields a different DNS label.

UTS #39 describes Unicode confusable detection:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr39/

inputs.name: real_world_url_default_port_explicit
inputs.description:
Port 443 is the default for HTTPS, so this spelling is equivalent to the
paired URL with no explicit port. Parsers that retain one spelling but not
the other can create duplicate cache or policy entries.

RFC 3986 section 6.2.3 covers scheme-based default-port normalization:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-6.2.3

inputs.name: real_world_url_default_port_implicit
inputs.description:
This HTTPS URL omits the default port and is paired with an explicit port
443 spelling. Scheme-based normalization permits the explicit default port
to be removed, which matters for cache keys, origins, and allowlists.

RFC 3986 section 6.2.3 describes default-port normalization:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-6.2.3

inputs.name: real_world_url_dot_segments
inputs.description:
Literal `.` and `..` path segments are removed by syntax-based
normalization, so this URL is equivalent to the paired normalized path.
Implementations should run a defined algorithm rather than repeatedly
replacing substrings.

RFC 3986 sections 5.2.4 and 6.2.2.3 define dot-segment removal:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-5.2.4

inputs.name: real_world_url_dot_segments_normalized
inputs.description:
This is the normalized path for the paired URL containing literal dot
segments. It is also paired as `tricky` with a percent-encoded dot-segment
spelling because WHATWG special-URL parsing recognizes encoded dots while
generic RFC normalization does not first decode reserved structure.

RFC 3986 section 5.2.4 and the WHATWG URL path state define the relevant
normalization algorithms:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-5.2.4
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#url-path-state

inputs.name: real_world_url_double_encoded_traversal
inputs.description:
One percent-decoding pass turns `%252e%252e%252f` into `%2e%2e%2f`; a
second turns it into `../`. Pipelines in which a proxy, router, and
application decode at different stages can therefore expose traversal that
no individual layer sees in the original text.

RFC 3986 section 2.4 warns that a percent-encoded string must not be decoded
more than once:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-2.4

inputs.name: real_world_url_empty_path
inputs.description:
This authority URL has an empty path. For HTTP-family retrieval and
scheme-based normalization it is equivalent to a path containing a single
slash, but raw string comparison treats the spellings as distinct.

RFC 3986 section 6.2.3 describes empty-path normalization:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-6.2.3

inputs.name: real_world_url_encoded_at_userinfo
inputs.description:
The percent-encoded at-sign belongs to the username; the later literal
at-sign terminates userinfo, so the host remains `attacker.invalid`.
Pre-decoding the whole URL before parsing creates two at-signs and can move
the perceived authority boundary.

RFC 3986 sections 2.4 and 3.2.1 require component boundaries to be parsed
before percent-decoding their data:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-2.4
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-3.2.1

inputs.name: real_world_url_encoded_dot_segment
inputs.description:
The WHATWG special-URL parser treats `%2e%2e` as a double-dot segment and
serializes this URL as `/a/b/marker`. A generic RFC 3986 normalizer does not
percent-decode a path before dot-segment removal, so parser choice changes
the normalized path.

The WHATWG URL Standard explicitly recognizes percent-encoded dot segments:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#double-dot-path-segment

inputs.name: real_world_url_encoded_nul
inputs.description:
`%00` is legal percent-encoded URL text but decodes to NUL. Passing the
decoded path through a C string API can truncate it before the visible
`.txt` suffix, while other layers retain the complete URL.

RFC 3986 section 2.1 defines percent-encoding and section 7.3 warns about
dangerous decoded octets and representation changes:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-7.3

inputs.name: real_world_url_encoded_slash
inputs.description:
`%2F` is an encoded reserved slash and remains data within one path segment
during generic URI normalization. Decoding it before routing changes the
segment structure and can produce authorization or traversal bugs.

RFC 3986 sections 2.2 and 6.2.2.2 say percent-encoded reserved characters are
not generally equivalent to their literal delimiters:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-2.2

inputs.name: real_world_url_fragment_looks_like_authority
inputs.description:
Everything after `#` is a fragment and is not part of the authority or HTTP
request target. UI truncation or display reordering can make the
attacker-shaped text look significant even though the actual host remains
`trusted.example`.

RFC 3986 sections 3.5 and 7.6 discuss fragments and semantic attacks:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-3.5
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-7.6

inputs.name: real_world_url_ideographic_dot_host
inputs.description:
The visible separator is U+3002 IDEOGRAPHIC FULL STOP rather than ASCII dot.
UTS #46 domain processing maps it to U+002E, so an IDNA-aware URL parser
produces the paired ASCII host. Byte-level suffix checks can miss it.

UTS #46 defines processing of mapped domain code points:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr46/#Processing

inputs.name: real_world_url_idn_a_label
inputs.description:
This URL uses the ASCII A-label form of the paired Japanese domain. An
IDNA-aware URL parser serializes the U-label input to this host, so the two
URLs identify the same origin and path.

RFC 5890 section 2.3.2.4 defines A-label/U-label relationships, and the
WHATWG host parser applies domain-to-ASCII processing:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5890.html#section-2.3.2.4
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#host-parsing

inputs.name: real_world_url_idn_u_label
inputs.description:
The Unicode U-label host serializes to the A-label in the paired URL. ASCII-
only validators may reject this presentation, while displaying Unicode
without spoofing protections can hide the canonical host.

RFC 5890 and the WHATWG URL Standard define the conversion:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5890.html#section-2.3.2.4
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-domain-to-ascii

inputs.name: real_world_url_ipv4_dotted_decimal
inputs.description:
This conventional TEST-NET-1 address is the baseline for non-canonical
integer and octal host spellings. The WHATWG IPv4 parser maps all three to
the same address, while RFC-oriented and IP-validation libraries may disagree.

The WHATWG URL Standard defines non-decimal IPv4 number parsing:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-ipv4-parser

inputs.name: real_world_url_ipv4_octal
inputs.description:
The leading zero makes the first IPv4 part octal under the WHATWG parser, so
`0300.0.2.1` becomes the TEST-NET address `192.0.2.1`. Other libraries have
historically treated leading-zero parts as decimal or rejected them, making
this a security-relevant parser differential for host allowlists.

The WHATWG IPv4 algorithm specifies octal prefixes. CPython's
CVE-2021-29921 tracked the security consequences of accepting ambiguous
leading-zero IPv4 text in `ipaddress`:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-ipv4-parser
https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/80565

inputs.name: real_world_url_ipv4_single_integer
inputs.description:
A WHATWG URL parser converts this single 32-bit integer host to `192.0.2.1`.
Validators that compare the source string to blocked dotted-decimal ranges
without canonicalizing can be bypassed.

The WHATWG URL Standard permits one-part IPv4 numbers:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-ipv4-parser

inputs.name: real_world_url_ipv6_compressed
inputs.description:
This URL uses the compressed spelling of an IPv6 documentation address.
Brackets delimit the IP literal from the port grammar, and the address is
numerically equivalent to the paired expanded form.

RFC 3986 section 3.2.2 defines bracketed IP literals, and RFC 5952 defines
canonical IPv6 text representation:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-3.2.2
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5952.html

inputs.name: real_world_url_ipv6_expanded
inputs.description:
This fully expanded IPv6 literal denotes the same address as the paired
compressed URL. Text comparison, cache keys, and host allowlists must account
for legal IPv6 presentation variants.

RFC 5952 describes canonicalization of IPv6 address text:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5952.html

inputs.name: real_world_url_leading_space
inputs.description:
The first byte is ASCII space before an otherwise absolute HTTPS URL.
WHATWG parsing strips leading C0 controls and spaces, while older CPython
`urllib.parse` behavior could classify this as a relative URL and let it
bypass checks that only inspected absolute URLs.

CPython tracked the security issue as CVE-2023-24329:
https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/102153
The WHATWG preprocessing rule is defined here:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#concept-basic-url-parser

inputs.name: real_world_url_literal_slash
inputs.description:
The literal slash creates two path segments and is not generically
equivalent to the paired `%2F` data octet. Servers that decode before
authorization may collapse these distinct resource names.

RFC 3986 section 2.2 defines slash as a reserved gen-delimiter:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-2.2

inputs.name: real_world_url_lone_percent
inputs.description:
The path ends in a percent sign without two hexadecimal digits. RFC 3986 does
not permit this as a `pct-encoded` token, while WHATWG parsing records a
validation error but preserves the URL for serialization. Strict and
browser-compatible validators therefore disagree.

RFC 3986 section 2.1 and the WHATWG percent-encoded-byte definition show the
differing error models:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-2.1
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#percent-encoded-bytes

inputs.name: real_world_url_maximum_dns_label
inputs.description:
The first host label is exactly 63 ASCII octets, the DNS maximum. This is a
valid boundary value that catches off-by-one checks and confusion between
Unicode input length and the post-IDNA A-label length.

RFC 1035 section 2.3.4 sets the 63-octet label limit:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1035.html#section-2.3.4

inputs.name: real_world_url_maximum_port
inputs.description:
Port 65535 is the largest valid TCP or UDP port number. It is a valid parser
boundary even though no service is expected at this reserved documentation
host.

The WHATWG URL port state rejects values greater than 65535:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#port-state

inputs.name: real_world_url_mixed_case_javascript_scheme
inputs.description:
URI schemes are case-insensitive, so this is a `javascript:` URL, not a
network fetch merely because it contains host-shaped authority text. Its
script body is a harmless line comment. Legacy Node.js `url.parse()` could
report a spoofed hostname when the scheme used mixed case, leading
applications to make incorrect security decisions.

Node.js published this as CVE-2018-12123:
https://nodejs.org/en/blog/vulnerability/november-2018-security-releases

inputs.name: real_world_url_network_path_reference
inputs.description:
This is a relative network-path reference, not an absolute URL because it has
no scheme. Resolving it requires a base URL and inherits that base's scheme;
validators that merely require a host may accidentally accept an
attacker-controlled cross-origin reference.

RFC 3986 sections 4.2 and 5 define relative references and resolution:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-4.2

inputs.name: real_world_url_overflowing_port
inputs.description:
Port 65536 is one above the maximum and must fail WHATWG URL parsing.
Implementations that store the value in 16 bits before range checking can
wrap it to zero, while lenient parsers may leave it as uninterpreted text.

The WHATWG URL port state defines the range check:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#port-state

inputs.name: real_world_url_oversized_dns_label
inputs.description:
The first host label is 64 ASCII octets, one over the DNS limit. Parsers may
accept and serialize it as host text even though strict DNS validation or
resolution must fail, so URL parser acceptance and usable web-host validity
can diverge.

RFC 1035 section 2.3.4 sets the label limit:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc1035.html#section-2.3.4

inputs.name: real_world_url_percent_encoded_dot_host
inputs.description:
The host parser decodes `%2E` to a dot before domain processing, so this is
equivalent to `example.com` in the paired URL. String-based suffix checks
performed before host canonicalization may miss the boundary.

The WHATWG host parser defines percent-decoding before domain parsing:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#host-parsing

inputs.name: real_world_url_percent_encoded_separator_host
inputs.description:
Current URL parsers should reject this host because `%2F` decodes to a path
separator. curl 7.80.0 through 7.83.0 accepted this shape and later
transposed it into a URL with a different host/path boundary, enabling filter
bypasses. The IP is TEST-NET-1, making this an inert regression case.

curl's CVE-2022-27780 advisory documents the parser flaw and example shape:
https://curl.se/docs/CVE-2022-27780.html

inputs.name: real_world_url_plain_dot_host
inputs.description:
This ordinary host is the normalized baseline for a percent-encoded dot in
the paired URL. Host parsing has component-specific percent-decoding rules
that must not be copied blindly to paths or queries.

The WHATWG host parser percent-decodes opaque host input before domain-to-
ASCII processing:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#host-parsing

inputs.name: real_world_url_query_crlf_injection
inputs.description:
The query contains percent-encoded CRLF followed by an inert marker header.
A URL library must not turn decoded query data into HTTP header structure.
The `.invalid` host prevents an accidental request from reaching a service.

CPython's CVE-2019-9740 allowed CRLF injection through attacker-controlled
URL query text passed to `urllib`:
https://github.com/python/cpython/issues/80457

inputs.name: real_world_url_query_duplicate_admin_first
inputs.description:
This reverses the duplicate values from the paired URL. Sorting query pairs
or storing them in a single-value map changes semantics for applications that
use first-value or last-value precedence.

The WHATWG `URLSearchParams` model preserves pair order and duplicate names:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#interface-urlsearchparams

inputs.name: real_world_url_query_duplicate_user_first
inputs.description:
The query contains the same key twice with `user` first and `admin` last.
Query syntax does not define duplicate-key resolution: frameworks may choose
first, last, all, or reject, so authorization code and business logic can
observe different values.

RFC 3986 section 3.4 treats the query as component data rather than defining
a key-value map:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-3.4

inputs.name: real_world_url_query_empty_value
inputs.description:
This query explicitly supplies an empty value with `flag=`. Form decoders
often produce the same pair as the missing-equals input, while signature
code, caches, and applications that inspect the raw query can distinguish
them.

The WHATWG form-urlencoded parser specifies empty-value handling:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#urlencoded-parsing

inputs.name: real_world_url_query_encoded_ampersand
inputs.description:
The encoded ampersand is data inside the `note` value, while the later
literal ampersand separates the `mode` field. Decoding the entire query
before splitting creates an extra apparent parameter.

RFC 3986 sections 2.4 and 3.4 describe safe parsing order and query data:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-2.4

inputs.name: real_world_url_query_missing_equals
inputs.description:
The field `flag` has no equals sign. `URLSearchParams`-style form parsing
yields an empty value, but raw-query consumers can distinguish presence-only
flags from explicitly empty values, making equality application-dependent.

The WHATWG form-urlencoded parser defines splitting names and values:
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#urlencoded-parsing

inputs.name: real_world_url_query_nested_url
inputs.description:
The value is itself a URL whose `?`, `=`, and `#` delimiters are
percent-encoded. Splitting or decoding the outer URL in the wrong order can
promote inner delimiters into the outer query or fragment.

RFC 3986 section 2.4 requires component boundaries to be established before
decoding percent-encoded data:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-2.4

inputs.name: real_world_url_query_percent20_space
inputs.description:
`%20` unambiguously represents a space octet in the query. It differs from
the paired literal plus under generic URI semantics, although HTML form
decoders commonly map both spellings to the same field value.

RFC 3986 sections 2.1 and 3.4 define percent-encoding and query syntax:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-2.1

inputs.name: real_world_url_query_plus_literal
inputs.description:
In generic URI query syntax, plus is a literal plus character. It becomes a
space only under the separate `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` decoding
convention, so generic URL normalization must not equate it with the paired
`%20` spelling.

RFC 3986 section 3.4 defines query syntax, while the HTML Standard defines
the form-specific plus-to-space behavior:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-3.4
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#application/x-www-form-urlencoded

inputs.name: real_world_url_query_semicolon_separator
inputs.description:
Generic URI syntax allows semicolon in a query but does not define it as a
field separator. Some legacy query parsers split on both semicolon and
ampersand while modern form parsers use ampersand, producing one field or two
from the same bytes.

RFC 3986 section 3.4 defines the generic query component:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-3.4

inputs.name: real_world_url_repeated_slash_path
inputs.description:
The doubled slash creates an empty path segment and is not generically equal
to the paired path. Servers and proxies often merge slashes anyway, which can
make access-control matching disagree with request routing.

Apache tracked unexpected URL matching with `MergeSlashes OFF` as
CVE-2021-30641, demonstrating that repeated separators affect real security
boundaries:
https://httpd.apache.org/security/vulnerabilities_24.html#CVE-2021-30641

inputs.name: real_world_url_root_path
inputs.description:
This explicit root path is the normalized HTTP-family form of the paired URL
whose path is empty. Serializers commonly add this slash even when it was
absent from the input.

RFC 3986 section 6.2.3 specifies the equivalence:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-6.2.3

inputs.name: real_world_url_scheme_host_lowercase
inputs.description:
This is the lowercase baseline for a case-normalization pair. URI schemes
and registered host names are case-insensitive, while path and query
components are not generally case-folded.

RFC 3986 sections 3.1, 3.2.2, and 6.2.2.1 define this normalization:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-6.2.2.1

inputs.name: real_world_url_scheme_host_uppercase
inputs.description:
The scheme and host differ from the paired URL only in ASCII case and are
equivalent. The mixed-case path is intentionally unchanged: lowercasing the
entire URL would corrupt a potentially case-sensitive resource identifier.

RFC 3986 section 6.2.2.1 limits case normalization to appropriate components:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-6.2.2.1

inputs.name: real_world_url_semicolon_traversal
inputs.description:
The segment `..;` is not an RFC dot segment, but frameworks that strip
semicolon path parameters before routing can turn it into `..`. Differences
between proxy normalization and application routing make this a useful
authorization and traversal regression case.

RFC 3986 sections 3.3 and 5.2.4 define path segments and exact dot-segment
matching:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-3.3
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-5.2.4

inputs.name: real_world_url_single_slash_path
inputs.description:
This single-separator path is paired with a double-slash spelling. RFC path
syntax preserves empty segments, so normalizers must not merge the pair
unless an application or server configuration explicitly defines that
behavior.

RFC 3986 section 3.3 defines slash-delimited path segments:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-3.3

inputs.name: real_world_url_special_scheme_without_slashes
inputs.description:
A WHATWG parser treats this as `http://example.com/path` even though `//` is
absent after the scheme. RFC 3986 parsing instead permits an
authority-less path beginning with `example.com`, so component extraction is
parser-dialect dependent.

Compare RFC 3986 hierarchical syntax with the WHATWG special authority
states:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-3
https://url.spec.whatwg.org/#special-authority-slashes-state

inputs.name: real_world_url_unreserved_literal
inputs.description:
The tilde is an unreserved URI character. It is the normalized literal form
of the paired percent-encoded spelling and must not be confused with decoding
reserved characters such as slash.

RFC 3986 sections 2.3 and 6.2.2.2 define this equivalence:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-6.2.2.2

inputs.name: real_world_url_unreserved_percent_encoded
inputs.description:
`%7E` encodes the unreserved tilde character and is equivalent to the paired
literal spelling. Canonicalizers may decode it without changing the path's
delimiter structure.

RFC 3986 section 6.2.2.2 permits decoding percent-encoded unreserved octets:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-6.2.2.2

inputs.name: real_world_url_userinfo_host_confusion
inputs.description:
The text before `@` is userinfo, not the host. The actual host is
`attacker.invalid`, even though the URL begins with a trusted-looking domain.
Display code and allowlists that stop at the first host-shaped token can make
the wrong security decision.

RFC 3986 section 3.2.1 defines userinfo and warns that it can be misleading:
https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986.html#section-3.2.1

inputs.name: soft_hyphen_in_word
inputs.description:
This is the word "automobile" with a SOFT HYPHEN (U+00AD) inserted after
"auto". A soft hyphen marks a permitted hyphenation point: it renders as
nothing when the word fits on the line, but appears as a visible hyphen glyph
if the renderer breaks the line there. The same bytes therefore render with
different width and a different glyph count depending on the surrounding
layout, and copying the displayed text can silently include or drop the
character. It tests conditional rendering and width that depends on line
breaking rather than on the text alone.

Unicode Standard Annex #14 describes SOFT HYPHEN line-break behaviour:
https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr14/

inputs.name: utf8_chunk_ascii_a
inputs.description:
A single ASCII letter is valid UTF-8 on its own. When appended to the paired
truncated EURO SIGN prefix, the concatenation E2 82 41 is invalid because
ASCII 0x41 appears where a continuation byte is required. This adjunct tests
validators that join independently supplied chunks.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: utf8_chunk_cent_sign
inputs.description:
CENT SIGN (U+00A2) is a complete valid two-byte UTF-8 string. Appending the
paired unexpected-continuation chunk produces C2 A2 80, which is invalid
because the complete character is followed by a continuation byte with no
starter. This adjunct tests validation across concatenated chunks.

See the Unicode well-formed UTF-8 byte sequences in Table 3-7:
https://www.unicode.org/versions/Unicode15.0.0/ch03.pdf

inputs.name: zero_width_invisibles
inputs.description:
This is a run of five code points that each render with zero advance width:
ZERO WIDTH SPACE (U+200B), ZERO WIDTH NON-JOINER (U+200C), ZERO WIDTH JOINER
(U+200D), WORD JOINER (U+2060), and ZERO WIDTH NO-BREAK SPACE / BOM (U+FEFF).
The decoded text is several code points long but paints nothing, so any layout
or width calculation keyed off byte or code-point count over-reports its size,
while cursor positioning and selection can land on positions that are visually
indistinguishable. Several are Default_Ignorable, so conforming renderers must
not show a "missing glyph" box for them.

Unicode lists the Default_Ignorable_Code_Point property in the Unicode
Character Database: https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr44/#Default_Ignorable_Code_Point
